abstract: A fundamental objective of manipulation policy design is to endow robots to comprehend human instructions, reason about scene cues, and execute generalized actions in dynamic environments. Recent autoregressive vision-language-action (VLA) methods inherit common-sense reasoning capabilities from vision-language models (VLMs) for next action-token prediction. However, these methods quantize actions into discrete bins, which disrupts the continuity required for precise control. In contrast, existing diffusion-based VLA methods incorporate an additional diffusion head to predict continuous actions solely conditioned on feature representations extracted by the VLM, without fully leveraging the VLM's pretrained reasoning capabilities through token-level generation. To address these limitations, we introduce HybridVLA, a unified framework that absorbs the continuous nature of diffusion-based actions and the contextual reasoning of autoregression within a single large language model. To mitigate interference between the two generation paradigms, we propose a collaborative training recipe that seamlessly incorporates diffusion denoising into the next-token prediction process. With this recipe, we find these two action prediction methods not only reinforce each other but also exhibit varying strength across different tasks. Therefore, we design a collaborative action ensemble mechanism that adaptively fuses both predictions, leading to more robust control. HybridVLA outperforms previous state-of-the-art VLA methods by 14\% and 19\% in mean success rate on simulation and real-world tasks, respectively, while demonstrating stable manipulation in unseen configurations.

keyInsights: Deeply integrating diffusion-based continuous action generation within the LLM's next-token prediction process enables full utilization of the VLM's pretrained reasoning for precise control.
A collaborative training recipe with hybrid objectives allows a single LLM backbone to simultaneously learn continuous action characteristics and semantic reasoning without negative interference.

Adaptive ensembling of continuous diffusion and discrete autoregressive actions, based on model confidence, leverages the complementary strengths of both paradigms for improved overall performance and robustness.
originalProblem: Autoregressive Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, while leveraging VLM reasoning, quantize continuous actions, leading to a loss of precision in robotic manipulation.
Existing diffusion-based VLA models often relegate the VLM to a feature extractor, thus failing to fully integrate the VLM's powerful token-level reasoning for continuous action prediction.
A fundamental challenge exists in synergistically combining precise continuous control with robust semantic understanding derived from large language models within a unified framework.
results: Achieved a 74% mean success rate across 10 RLBench simulation tasks, outperforming previous state-of-the-art autoregressive models (OpenVLA by 33%) and diffusion models (CogACT by 14%).
Demonstrated robust performance in real-world robotic tasks, achieving 83% mean success for single-arm and 71% for dual-arm tasks, representing a 19% improvement over prior VLA methods.
Exhibited strong generalization capabilities, maintaining high performance across unseen manipulated objects, backgrounds, spatial positions, and lighting conditions.
solution: HybridVLA unifies diffusion and autoregressive action generation within a single Large Language Model (LLM) backbone, building on a pretrained VLM.

A "collaborative training recipe" is used, involving a novel token sequence design (e.g., encapsulating diffusion tokens) and joint optimization of diffusion and autoregressive losses to enable mutual reinforcement.

During inference, a "collaborative action ensemble" adaptively fuses continuous diffusion-based actions with discrete autoregressive actions, based on autoregressive confidence, to balance precision and reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: HybridVLA introduces a unified Vision-Language-Action model that integrates both diffusion-based continuous action prediction and autoregressive reasoning within a shared Large Language Model backbone. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in robotic manipulation, demonstrating up to 19% higher success rates over prior methods in real-world tasks and strong generalization to unseen conditions.

title: HybridVLA: Collaborative Diffusion and Autoregression in a Unified Vision-Language-Action Model

abstract:
Building autonomous robotic agents capable of achieving human-level
performance in real-world embodied tasks is an ultimate goal in humanoid robot
research. Recent advances have made significant progress in high-level
cognition with Foundation Models (FMs) and low-level skill development for
humanoid robots. However, directly combining these components often results in
poor robustness and efficiency due to compounding errors in long-horizon tasks
and the varied latency of different modules. We introduce Being-0, a
hierarchical agent framework that integrates an FM with a modular skill
library. The FM handles high-level cognitive tasks such as instruction
understanding, task planning, and reasoning, while the skill library provides
stable locomotion and dexterous manipulation for low-level control. To bridge
the gap between these levels, we propose a novel Connector module, powered by a
lightweight vision-language model (VLM). The Connector enhances the FM's
embodied capabilities by translating language-based plans into actionable skill
commands and dynamically coordinating locomotion and manipulation to improve
task success. With all components, except the FM, deployable on low-cost
onboard computation devices, Being-0 achieves efficient, real-time performance
on a full-sized humanoid robot equipped with dexterous hands and active vision.
Extensive experiments in large indoor environments demonstrate Being-0's
effectiveness in solving complex, long-horizon tasks that require challenging
navigation and manipulation subtasks. For further details and videos, visit
this https URL

keyInsights: An efficient, onboard VLM can effectively bridge the "mind-body" gap between abstract FM plans and real-time physical actions, overcoming latency and embodied grounding issues critical for humanoids.

Dynamic pose adjustment during navigation and active camera control are crucial for achieving seamless transitions between different task phases and ensuring high success rates in precise manipulation.

A distributed computational architecture, leveraging cloud for high-level reasoning and onboard processing for embodied decision-making, significantly enhances the robustness and efficiency of humanoid agents.
originalProblem: Integrating powerful, slow cloud-based Foundation Models (FMs) with fast, low-level humanoid robot skills leads to poor robustness and efficiency for complex, long-horizon tasks.
Humanoid robots face unique challenges like inherent bipedal instability and FMs' limited 3D spatial understanding, which hinder accurate navigation and manipulation planning.
Existing systems struggle to seamlessly chain diverse skills for long-horizon tasks, often resulting in suboptimal pose transitions between navigation and manipulation that lead to failures.
results: Being-0 achieved an 84.4% average completion rate on challenging long-horizon tasks, with the Connector improving performance (e.g., Fetch-bottle from 0% to 90% success rate) compared to baselines.
The Connector module increased average navigation speed by 4.2x (9.6 cm/s vs. 2.3 cm/s) while maintaining a perfect success rate, demonstrating improved efficiency and robustness for navigation.

Ablation studies confirmed that pose adjustment improved grasping success rates (e.g., Grasp-coffee from 1/5 to 4/5), and active vision enabled consistent 5/5 success across all navigation and manipulation tasks, unlike fixed camera setups.
solution: A hierarchical agent framework is proposed, featuring a cloud-based Foundation Model for high-level planning, an onboard Vision-Language Model (VLM) "Connector," and a modular skill library.

The VLM-based Connector module acts as an intelligent intermediary, translating FM plans into grounded skill commands, handling real-time visual navigation, and adjusting robot pose for seamless skill transitions.

The system leverages reinforcement learning for stable locomotion and imitation learning with teleoperation for acquiring dexterous manipulation skills, complemented by active vision for enhanced perception.
paper_summary.summary: Being-0, a full-sized humanoid robot agent, successfully performs complex, multi-step tasks in real-world environments. Its framework integrates a high-level Foundation Model with robust modular skills via a novel VLM-based "Connector" module, which enhances embodied scene understanding, real-time control, and efficient task execution.

title: Being-0: A Humanoid Robotic Agent with Vision-Language Models and  Modular Skills

abstract: Embodied long-horizon manipulation requires robotic systems to process multimodal inputs-such as vision and natural language-and translate them into executable actions. However, existing learning-based approaches often depend on large, task-specific datasets and struggle to generalize to unseen scenarios. Recent methods have explored using large language models (LLMs) as high-level planners that decompose tasks into subtasks using natural language and guide pretrained low-level controllers. Yet, these approaches assume perfect execution from low-level policies, which is unrealistic in real-world environments with noise or suboptimal behaviors. To overcome this, we fully discard the pretrained low-level policy and instead use the LLM to directly generate executable code plans within a closed-loop framework. Our planner employs chain-of-thought (CoT)-guided few-shot learning with incrementally structured examples to produce robust and generalizable task plans. Complementing this, a reporter evaluates outcomes using RGB-D and delivers structured feedback, enabling recovery from misalignment and replanning under partial observability. This design eliminates per-step inference, reduces computational overhead, and limits error accumulation that was observed in previous methods. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on 30+ diverse seen and unseen long-horizon tasks across LoHoRavens, CALVIN, Franka Kitchen, and cluttered real-world settings.

keyInsights: Direct LLM-based code generation, coupled with a robust closed-loop feedback mechanism, can effectively replace data-intensive pretrained low-level policies for long-horizon robotic manipulation, achieving high performance and data-agnosticism.

Structured, incrementally difficult few-shot examples augmented with Chain-of-Thought reasoning significantly enhance an LLM's ability to generalize to novel and complex tasks by guiding its logical understanding.

VLM-based structured contextual feedback is critical for real-world reliability, enabling the robotic system to detect errors, adapt to partial observability (e.g., occluded objects), and iteratively replan for successful task completion.
originalProblem: Traditional Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models require massive, costly datasets for pretraining and fine-tuning, limiting their scalability and generalization to novel robotic tasks.

Hierarchical frameworks that use LLMs for high-level planning and pre-trained low-level policies often struggle with real-world noise and perturbations due to the 'perfect execution' assumption of these policies, leading to cumulative errors.

Existing LLM-based code generation methods for robotics typically operate in an open-loop manner, lacking robust feedback for error recovery, and rely on randomly selected examples for few-shot learning, which is ineffective for complex generalization.
results: DAHLIA achieved state-of-the-art success rates on the LoHoRavens benchmark, including 100% on five original tasks and consistently highest performance on 10 newly generated, unseen tasks, with closed-loop feedback improving performance by up to 24% over its planner-only variant.

The framework demonstrated strong scalability and generalization across different robot embodiments (UR5e and Franka arm) and task setups (CALVIN and Franka Kitchen benchmarks), achieving 100% success in several tasks and up to 8% improvements in precise dexterous manipulation.

In real-world demonstrations, DAHLIA successfully completed four challenging tasks, including handling an initially occluded object by detecting its exposure via the VLM reporter and initiating successful re-planning, showcasing robust error recovery and adaptability to dynamic environments.
solution: DAHLIA introduces a dual-tunnel closed-loop framework where a 'Planner' (LLM) generates executable Python code directly from human instructions, while a 'Reporter' (VLM) provides structured contextual feedback after each execution loop.

It implements Chain-of-Thought (CoT)-guided few-shot adaptation using an incrementally structured curriculum of examples, teaching the LLM robust reasoning patterns for better generalization to unseen tasks.

The framework leverages temporal abstraction by executing entire sequences of generated motion primitives within a loop before seeking feedback, thereby reducing per-step inference overhead and limiting error accumulation.
paper_summary.summary: The DAHLIA framework enables robots to perform complex, multi-step manipulation tasks by generating executable code directly from natural language instructions. It employs a closed-loop dual-tunnel system with incremental few-shot adaptation, achieving high success rates on various benchmarks and demonstrating robust error recovery in real-world scenarios without relying on extensive pre-trained policies.

title: Embodied Long Horizon Manipulation with Closed-loop Code Generation and Incremental Few-shot Adaptation

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities in the
hierarchical decomposition of complex tasks through semantic reasoning.
However, their application in embodied systems faces challenges in ensuring
reliable execution of subtask sequences and achieving one-shot success in
long-term task completion. To address these limitations in dynamic
environments, we propose Closed-Loop Embodied Agent (CLEA) -- a novel
architecture incorporating four specialized open-source LLMs with functional
decoupling for closed-loop task management. The framework features two core
innovations: (1) Interactive task planner that dynamically generates executable
subtasks based on the environmental memory, and (2) Multimodal execution critic
employing an evaluation framework to conduct a probabilistic assessment of
action feasibility, triggering hierarchical re-planning mechanisms when
environmental perturbations exceed preset thresholds. To validate CLEA's
effectiveness, we conduct experiments in a real environment with manipulable
objects, using two heterogeneous robots for object search, manipulation, and
search-manipulation integration tasks. Across 12 task trials, CLEA outperforms
the baseline model, achieving a 67.3% improvement in success rate and a 52.8%
increase in task completion rate. These results demonstrate that CLEA
significantly enhances the robustness of task planning and execution in dynamic
environments.

author_info.institution: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
Shenzhen Future Network of Intelligence Institute

keyInsights: Functional decoupling of LLMs enables more robust and adaptable behavior
Real-time execution criticism significantly improves task completion success
Environmental memory helps maintain context for long-horizon tasks
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based robotic systems struggle to adapt to dynamic environments and unexpected changes
Open-loop architectures lack real-time feedback integration and robust error recovery
results: Achieved 67.3% improvement in success rate over baseline systems
Demonstrated 52.8% increase in task completion rate
Successfully validated on multiple robotic platforms in kitchen environment
Proved effectiveness through ablation studies of individual components
solution: Developed CLEA, a closed-loop framework integrating four specialized LLMs with distinct roles
Implemented interactive task planning with real-time environmental feedback and execution criticism
Created environmental memory system for maintaining contextual awareness
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from CUHK-Shenzhen and Shanghai Jiao Tong University introduce CLEA, a groundbreaking closed-loop framework that enables robust LLM-based robotic task planning through four specialized language models working in concert, achieving a 67.3% improvement in success rate over baseline systems while demonstrating practical deployment in dynamic real-world environments.

title: CLEA: Closed-Loop Embodied Agent for Enhancing Task Execution in Dynamic  Environments

abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems have shown strong potential for language-driven robotic manipulation. However, scaling them to long-horizon tasks remains challenging. Existing pipelines typically separate data collection, policy learning, and deployment, resulting in heavy reliance on manual environment resets and brittle multi-policy execution. We present RoboClaw, an agentic robotics framework that unifies data collection, policy learning, and task execution under a single VLM-driven controller. At the policy level, RoboClaw introduces Entangled Action Pairs (EAP), which couple forward manipulation behaviors with inverse recovery actions to form self-resetting loops for autonomous data collection. This mechanism enables continuous on-policy data acquisition and iterative policy refinement with minimal human intervention. During deployment, the same agent performs high-level reasoning and dynamically orchestrates learned policy primitives to accomplish long-horizon tasks. By maintaining consistent contextual semantics across collection and execution, RoboClaw reduces mismatch between the two phases and improves multi-policy robustness. Experiments in real-world manipulation tasks demonstrate improved stability and scalability compared to conventional open-loop pipelines, while significantly reducing human effort throughout the robot lifecycle, achieving a 25% improvement in success rate over baseline methods on long-horizon tasks and reducing human time investment by 53.7%.

keyInsights: Unifying the entire robot policy lifecycle under a single VLM-driven agent creates consistent contextual semantics and decision logic, reducing human intervention and enhancing robustness.
The Entangled Action Pairs (EAP) mechanism significantly reduces human effort and time by enabling autonomous self-resetting loops for continuous on-policy data collection.

Continuous process-level supervision and dynamic recovery mechanisms during deployment allow for robust execution of complex, long-horizon tasks by adapting to failures and refining policies over time.
originalProblem: High human involvement is required for data collection, environment resets, failure monitoring, and supervision in current robot learning pipelines.
Long-horizon tasks suffer from brittle multi-policy execution, where errors accumulate and propagate, making the overall process fragile.
Semantic and distribution mismatches arise when data collection, policy learning, and deployment stages are handled separately, leading to inconsistencies in task interpretation and success criteria.
results: RoboClaw reduced human time for data collection by 53.7% and required 8.04 times less human intervention during rollouts compared to purely manual baselines.
The framework demonstrated a 25% improvement in success rate for long-horizon tasks on real-world vanity table organization compared to baseline methods.

Individual subtask policy success rates steadily improved (e.g., Body Lotion from 21/50 to 43/50) with iteratively collected data, and the system learned from failures to perform autonomous recovery strategies.
solution: A VLM-driven meta-controller performs high-level decision-making through in-context learning, reasoning over environmental observations and structured memory to unify data collection, policy learning, and task execution.

Self-resetting data collection is achieved via Entangled Action Pairs (EAP), where each forward manipulation policy is complemented by an inverse recovery policy, enabling continuous, autonomous online data generation.

Deployment-time process supervision and dynamic skill scheduling allow the agent to continuously monitor subtask progress, dynamically invoke recovery policies for failures, and escalate to human intervention when autonomous recovery is not possible.
paper_summary.summary: RoboClaw introduces an agentic framework that unifies data collection, policy learning, and execution for long-horizon robotic tasks, leveraging a Vision-Language Model as a meta-controller. This framework decreased human time for data collection by 53.7% and improved long-horizon task success rates by 25% compared to baseline methods on real-world manipulation tasks.

title: RoboClaw: An Agentic Framework for Scalable Long-Horizon Robotic Tasks

abstract: Humanoid loco-manipulation, which integrates whole-body locomotion with dexterous manipulation, remains a fundamental challenge in robotics. Beyond whole-body coordination and balance, a central difficulty lies in understanding human instructions and translating them into coherent sequences of embodied actions. Recent advances in foundation models provide transferable multimodal representations and reasoning capabilities, yet existing efforts remain largely restricted to either locomotion or manipulation in isolation, with limited applicability to humanoid settings. In this paper, we propose Humanoid-COA, the first humanoid agent framework that integrates foundation model reasoning with an Embodied Chain-of-Action (CoA) mechanism for zero-shot loco-manipulation. Within the perception--reasoning--action paradigm, our key contribution lies in the reasoning stage, where the proposed CoA mechanism decomposes high-level human instructions into structured sequences of locomotion and manipulation primitives through affordance analysis, spatial inference, and whole-body action reasoning. Extensive experiments on two humanoid robots, Unitree H1-2 and G1, in both an open test area and an apartment environment, demonstrate that our framework substantially outperforms prior baselines across manipulation, locomotion, and loco-manipulation tasks, achieving robust generalization to long-horizon and unstructured scenarios. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating object affordance, spatial reasoning, and whole-body movement inference within a Chain-of-Action reasoning mechanism is crucial for robust, physically grounded humanoid loco-manipulation.
Multimodal foundation models, when appropriately grounded through embodied reasoning, can enable zero-shot execution of complex, long-horizon tasks in unstructured real-world environments.
Iterative reasoning and plan adaptation based on new observations significantly improve humanoid reliability and adaptability in dynamic and partially observable scenarios.
originalProblem: Integrating whole-body locomotion with dexterous manipulation for humanoid robots remains a challenge in real-world, high-dimensional scenarios.
Translating abstract natural language instructions into physically feasible, low-level control for complex humanoid platforms often leads to brittleness or limited scalability.

Existing robotic planning methods typically treat locomotion and manipulation in isolation, struggle with zero-shot generalization, or underutilize advanced reasoning capabilities of large language models.
results: Humanoid-COA consistently outperformed three baseline methods across simple and complex manipulation, locomotion, and combined loco-manipulation tasks on real-world Unitree H1-2 and G1 humanoid robots.

The framework achieved success rates up to 96.6% for simple tasks and maintained over 60% success for highly complex, long-horizon, and occlusion-aware loco-manipulation scenarios, significantly exceeding baseline performances which often failed (e.g., 0% for some baselines).

An ablation study confirmed that all three components of the Embodied Chain-of-Action Reasoning (object affordance, spatial reasoning, whole-body movement inference) incrementally contribute to the framework's superior executability and success rates.
solution: A novel Humanoid-COA framework integrates multimodal foundation models (GPT-4V for perception, GPT-4 for reasoning) within a perception-reasoning-action paradigm.
Its core is an Embodied Chain-of-Action (CoA) Reasoning mechanism, which iteratively decomposes natural language instructions into executable action sequences.

CoA Reasoning explicitly incorporates object affordance analysis, region spatial reasoning, and whole-body movement inference to ensure physical feasibility, spatial awareness, and kinematic consistency for generated actions.
paper_summary.summary: The Humanoid-COA framework enables humanoid robots to execute complex loco-manipulation tasks from natural language instructions in a zero-shot manner, leveraging multimodal foundation models. It consistently surpassed baseline methods across manipulation, locomotion, and combined tasks, demonstrating robust performance in real-world, unstructured environments.

title: Humanoid Agent via Embodied Chain-of-Action Reasoning with Multimodal Foundation Models for Zero-Shot Loco-Manipulation

abstract: The development of general robotic systems capable of manipulating in unstructured environments is a significant challenge. While Vision-Language Models(VLM) excel in high-level commonsense reasoning, they lack the fine-grained 3D spatial understanding required for precise manipulation tasks. Fine-tuning VLM on robotic datasets to create Vision-Language-Action Models(VLA) is a potential solution, but it is hindered by high data collection costs and generalization issues. To address these challenges, we propose a novel object-centric representation that bridges the gap between VLM's high-level reasoning and the low-level precision required for manipulation. Our key insight is that an object's canonical space, defined by its functional affordances, provides a structured and semantically meaningful way to describe interaction primitives, such as points and directions. These primitives act as a bridge, translating VLM's commonsense reasoning into actionable 3D spatial constraints. In this context, we introduce a dual closed-loop, open-vocabulary robotic manipulation system: one loop for high-level planning through primitive resampling, interaction rendering and VLM checking, and another for low-level execution via 6D pose tracking. This design ensures robust, real-time control without requiring VLM fine-tuning. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong zero-shot generalization across diverse robotic manipulation tasks, highlighting the potential of this approach for automating large-scale simulation data generation.

keyInsights: An object's canonical space, defined by functional affordances, provides structured way to describe interaction primitives
Interaction primitives in canonical space bridge VLM reasoning with precise 3D spatial constraints
Self-correction through rendering and resampling enables closed-loop reasoning and mitigates VLM hallucination issues
originalProblem: Current VLMs lack fine-grained 3D spatial understanding needed for precise manipulation
Fine-tuning VLMs on robotic datasets is costly and limits generalization
Existing methods for defining and using interaction primitives are task-agnostic and unstable
results: Demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization across diverse manipulation tasks
Achieved over 15% improvement in performance with closed-loop planning
System proved effective for automated generation of demonstration data for behavior cloning
Showed robustness across different viewpoints compared to baseline methods
solution: Introduces object-centric interaction primitives defined in canonical space based on object functionality
Implements a dual closed-loop system: one for planning through primitive resampling and VLM checking, another for execution via pose tracking
Uses off-the-shelf models for universal 6D pose estimation and grasping without requiring VLM fine-tuning
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents a novel object-centric representation system for bridging high-level vision-language models with precise robotic manipulation

title: OmniManip: Towards General Robotic Manipulation via Object-Centric Interaction Primitives as Spatial Constraints

abstract: Equipping embodied agents with the ability to reason about tasks, foresee physical outcomes, and generate precise actions is essential for general-purpose manipulation. While recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have leveraged pre-trained foundation models, they typically focus on either linguistic planning or visual forecasting in isolation. These methods rarely integrate both capabilities simultaneously to guide action generation, leading to suboptimal performance in complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose BagelVLA, a unified model that integrates linguistic planning, visual forecasting, and action generation within a single framework. Initialized from a pretrained unified understanding and generative model, BagelVLA is trained to interleave textual reasoning and visual prediction directly into the action execution loop. To efficiently couple these modalities, we introduce Residual Flow Guidance (RFG), which initializes from current observation and leverages single-step denoising to extract predictive visual features, guiding action generation with minimal latency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BagelVLA outperforms existing baselines by a significant margin on multiple simulated and real-world benchmarks, particularly in tasks requiring multi-stage reasoning.

keyInsights: Explicitly interleaving linguistic planning, visual forecasting, and action generation within a unified framework provides substantial benefits for handling complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks.

The Residual Flow Guidance (RFG) mechanism allows for efficient and low-latency visual foresight by focusing on dynamic scene changes, making sophisticated visual prediction practical for real-time robotic control.
Leveraging large-scale, pre-trained unified understanding and generation models (like Bagel) provides a strong foundation for robust semantic generalization and multimodal reasoning in embodied AI.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often fragment capabilities, focusing on high-level planning or visual prediction in isolation, limiting performance in complex, multi-stage tasks.
Current methods lack seamless, interactive integration of linguistic reasoning and visual prediction to directly guide low-level control for long-horizon manipulation.
High latency associated with explicit visual generation in the action loop makes real-time visual foresight impractical for sophisticated robotic control.
results: Achieved an average task completion length of 4.405 on the Calvin (ABC-D split) benchmark, outperforming baselines like π0 (3.648) and UP-VLA (4.078).
Attained 75.26% success in the Robotwin Clean setting and 20.87% in the Randomized setting, surpassing π0 (46.42% Clean, 16.34% Random) and UP-VLA (52.92% Clean, 15.16% Random).

Demonstrated 73.3% average success for 'Stack Cubes' and 63.3% for 'Calculate and Place Symbol Blocks' on a real-world bimanual robot, showing higher success and planning accuracy compared to π0 and VPP.
solution: Proposes 'Interleaved Planning' which factorizes task execution into sequential linguistic planning (subtask generation), visual forecasting (keyframe prediction), and action generation steps within a unified Mixture-of-Transformers architecture.

Introduces Residual Flow Guidance (RFG) for efficient visual forecasting, which initializes image noise with the current frame's prior to predict residual changes, significantly reducing latency during action generation.
Employs a two-stage training strategy leveraging a hybrid dataset of robotic and general multimodal data to build and adapt multimodal reasoning capabilities for embodied control.
paper_summary.summary: BagelVLA, developed by researchers from Tsinghua University and ByteDance Seed, introduces a unified Vision-Language-Action framework that integrates linguistic planning, visual forecasting, and action generation within a single architecture. The model enhances performance in complex, long-horizon manipulation tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results across various simulated and real-world benchmarks.

title: BagelVLA: Enhancing Long-Horizon Manipulation via Interleaved Vision-Language-Action Generation

abstract:
Real-world embodied agents face long-horizon tasks, characterized by
high-level goals demanding multi-step solutions beyond single actions.
Successfully navigating these requires both high-level task planning (i.e.,
decomposing goals into sub-tasks) and low-level motion control (i.e.,
generating precise robot actions). While existing vision language action (VLA)
models and hierarchical architectures offer potential in embodied tasks, the
former often falter in planning, and the latter can suffer from coordination
issues, both hampering performance. We introduce a new unified VLA framework
for long-horizon tasks, dubbed LoHoVLA, to overcome these limitations. LoHoVLA
leverages a large pretrained vision language model (VLM) as the backbone to
jointly generate language and action tokens for sub-task generation and robot
action prediction, respectively. This shared representation promotes better
generalization across tasks. Additionally, LoHoVLA embraces a hierarchical
closed-loop control mechanism to mitigate errors originating from both
high-level planning and low-level control. To train LoHoVLA, we introduce
LoHoSet, a dataset built on the Ravens simulator, containing 20 long-horizon
tasks, each with 1,000 expert demonstrations composed of visual observations,
linguistic goals, sub-tasks, and robot actions. Experimental results show that
LoHoVLA significantly surpasses both hierarchical and standard VLA approaches
on long-horizon embodied tasks in the Ravens simulator. These findings
underscore the promise of unified architectures for advancing generalizable
embodied intelligence.

keyInsights: Unified architectures with shared VLM backbones can effectively integrate planning and control while maintaining generalization capabilities across diverse tasks
Hierarchical closed-loop control reduces computational overhead by avoiding unnecessary sub-task re-planning for transient low-level execution errors
Two-stage training (first optimizing text loss for planning, then both text and action losses) is crucial for balancing abstract reasoning and concrete action generation
originalProblem: Existing VLA models fall short in effective planning and reasoning for long-horizon tasks, relying on implicit sub-task planning that lacks interpretability and reliability
Hierarchical architectures suffer from suboptimal coordination and limited generalization between distinct planning and control modules, leading to error propagation and communication inefficiencies
results: Achieves 85.1% average reward and 81.0% success rate on complex reasoning tasks versus 8.2% for best hierarchical baseline
Demonstrates strong generalization to unseen tasks while requiring fewer high-level planning steps compared to strategies that re-plan after every failure
Shows that dataset expansion beyond original 4 LoHoRavens tasks is essential for preventing overfitting and enabling generalization to new task configurations
solution: Develop a unified VLA framework that jointly predicts both linguistic sub-tasks and robot actions using a shared VLM backbone (PaliGemma-3B) with augmented language head
Implement hierarchical closed-loop control that intelligently distinguishes between planning errors and execution errors, re-planning sub-tasks only when consecutive failures exceed threshold K=2
Create LoHoSet synthetic dataset with 20 long-horizon tasks and explicit sub-task annotations to enable proper training of the unified model
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, ShanghaiTech University, and Shanghai Jiao Tong University develop LoHoVLA, a unified Vision-Language-Action model that integrates high-level task planning and low-level motion control within a single framework, achieving 85.1% success on complex reasoning tasks like "put-even-blocks-in-same-color-zone" versus 8.2% for hierarchical baselines through joint prediction of linguistic sub-tasks and discrete action tokens using an augmented PaliGemma-3B backbone, while introducing a hierarchical closed-loop control mechanism that triggers sub-task re-planning only after consecutive failures exceed a threshold (K=2), thereby reducing redundant high-level planning steps, and creating LoHoSet, a synthetic dataset of 20 diverse long-horizon tasks with explicit sub-task annotations that enables two-stage training for optimal performance across both planning and control capabilities.

title: LoHoVLA: A Unified Vision-Language-Action Model for Long-Horizon  Embodied Tasks

abstract: Grounding natural-language instructions into continuous control for quadruped robots remains a fundamental challenge in vision language action. Existing methods struggle to bridge high-level semantic reasoning and low-level actuation, leading to unstable grounding and weak generalization in the real world. To address these issues, we present MobileVLA-R1, a unified vision-language-action framework that enables explicit reasoning and continuous control for quadruped robots. We construct MobileVLA-CoT, a large-scale dataset of multi-granularity chain-of-thought (CoT) for embodied trajectories, providing structured reasoning supervision for alignment. Built upon this foundation, we introduce a two-stage training paradigm that combines supervised CoT alignment with GRPO reinforcement learning to enhance reasoning consistency, control stability, and long-horizon execution. Extensive evaluations on VLN and VLA tasks demonstrate superior performance over strong baselines, with approximately a 5% improvement. Real-world deployment on a quadruped robot validates robust performance in complex environments. Code: this https URL. Website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning is effective for bridging the semantic understanding and continuous motor control gap in quadruped robots, improving interpretability and grounding stability.
A combined supervised fine-tuning and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) approach proves robust in learning reasoning consistency and stable continuous control for long-horizon tasks.
Integrating rich multimodal perception (RGB, depth, and point cloud) significantly enhances a mobile robot's ability to perform grounded navigation and interaction in complex 3D environments.
originalProblem: Existing robot control methods struggle to bridge the gap between high-level semantic reasoning from natural language and low-level continuous motor control.
Many current approaches lack transparent reasoning structures, using opaque latent embeddings that hinder interpretability, compositional reasoning, and error traceability for complex tasks.
Quadruped robots, in particular, require robust integration of complex locomotion and interaction skills with high-level cognitive abilities.
results: MobileVLA-R1 achieved state-of-the-art performance on VLN-CE benchmarks, with a 68.3% Success Rate (SR) and 65.2% Success-weighted Path Length (SPL) on R2R-CE Val-Unseen.
On the QUARD dataset, the framework demonstrated an average success rate of 0.73 across locomotion and manipulation tasks, outperforming strong baselines like QUART (0.44) and MoRE (0.60).
Real-world deployment on a Unitree Go2 quadruped robot showed robust performance, consistently outperforming NaVILA and GPT-4o, achieving a 0.91 SR for complex tasks in a Workspace environment.
solution: MobileVLA-R1 employs a hierarchical reasoning–execution paradigm, generating explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) action plans from multimodal observations before translating them into continuous control commands.

A large-scale, multi-granularity MobileVLA-CoT dataset is synthesized to provide structured reasoning supervision, explicitly linking natural language instructions to robot actions at various granularities.

A two-stage training paradigm combines supervised fine-tuning (SFT) for CoT alignment with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for reinforcement learning, using movement, action, and format rewards to refine policy and control stability.
paper_summary.summary: MobileVLA-R1 from Peking University introduces a framework enabling quadruped robots to execute natural-language instructions through explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoning and continuous control. The approach achieves state-of-the-art results on navigation and control benchmarks, including a 68.3% success rate on R2R-CE, and demonstrates robust real-world deployment on a Unitree Go2 robot.

title: MobileVLA-R1: Reinforcing Vision-Language-Action for Mobile Robots

abstract:
Language-guided long-horizon mobile manipulation has long been a grand challenge in embodied semantic reasoning, generalizable manipulation, and adaptive locomotion. Three fundamental limitations hinder progress: First, although large language models have improved spatial reasoning and task planning through semantic priors, existing implementations remain confined to tabletop scenarios, failing to address the constrained perception and limited actuation ranges of mobile platforms. Second, current manipulation strategies exhibit insufficient generalization when confronted with the diverse object configurations encountered in open-world environments. Third, while crucial for practical deployment, the dual requirement of maintaining high platform maneuverability alongside precise end-effector control in unstructured settings remains understudied.
In this work, we present ODYSSEY, a unified mobile manipulation framework for agile quadruped robots equipped with manipulators, which seamlessly integrates high-level task planning with low-level whole-body control. To address the challenge of egocentric perception in language-conditioned tasks, we introduce a hierarchical planner powered by a vision-language model, enabling long-horizon instruction decomposition and precise action execution. At the control level, our novel whole-body policy achieves robust coordination across challenging terrains. We further present the first benchmark for long-horizon mobile manipulation, evaluating diverse indoor and outdoor scenarios. Through successful sim-to-real transfer, we demonstrate the system's generalization and robustness in real-world deployments, underscoring the practicality of legged manipulators in unstructured environments. Our work advances the feasibility of generalized robotic assistants capable of complex, dynamic tasks. Our project page: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: Effectively integrating high-level semantic reasoning from LLMs/VLMs with low-level physical control is critical for autonomous, language-guided mobile manipulation in open-world settings.
A hierarchical planning approach that bridges semantic scene understanding with geometry-constrained local action guidance significantly enhances long-horizon task decomposition and execution.

Training a unified whole-body policy with terrain-invariant end-effector sampling improves robustness and generalization for combined locomotion and manipulation across diverse and challenging terrains.
originalProblem: Large language and vision-language models were confined to controlled tabletop scenarios, struggling with the constrained perception and limited actuation of mobile platforms in real-world environments.
Existing manipulation strategies lacked generalization to diverse object configurations and environmental variabilities found in open-world settings.

The crucial challenge of simultaneously maintaining high platform maneuverability (locomotion) and precise end-effector control (manipulation) for mobile manipulators in unstructured environments remained understudied.
A lack of comprehensive benchmarks hindered the systematic evaluation and progress of long-horizon mobile manipulation tasks.
results: ODYSSEY achieved over 40% overall task success and over 60% success in individual atomic actions across 8 complex long-horizon tasks in simulation.

The system demonstrated superior generalization to novel object configurations on short-horizon ARNOLD tasks, outperforming a strong end-to-end imitation learning baseline, PerAct, particularly in reorientation and cabinet manipulation.
Successful sim-to-real transfer was achieved on a physical quadruped robot, executing language-guided tasks like 'navigate to pick' and 'pick and place' with diverse objects.
solution: A coarse-to-fine task planner integrates LLM-based global planning over a semantic instance map with VLM-guided local manipulation via geometry-constrained end-effector pose estimation.
A learning-based unified whole-body control policy for quadruped robots is developed using a two-stage curriculum and terrain-invariant end-effector sampling for robust locomotion and manipulation.
A novel, comprehensive simulation benchmark is introduced, featuring diverse interactive assets, realistic scenes, and multi-stage tasks (including adapted ARNOLD and new long-horizon challenges).
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Zhejiang University and collaborators developed ODYSSEY, a framework enabling quadruped robots with manipulators to execute complex, language-guided tasks in open-world environments. It combines hierarchical planning with a learning-based whole-body control system, achieving over 40% task success on long-horizon challenges and demonstrating successful real-world deployment.

title: ODYSSEY: Open-World Quadrupeds Exploration and Manipulation for Long-Horizon Tasks

abstract: Although LLMs demonstrate proficiency in several text-based reasoning and planning tasks, their implementation in robotics control is constrained by significant deficiencies: (1) LLM agents are designed to work mainly with textual inputs rather than visual conditions; (2) Current multimodal agents treat LLMs as static planners, which separates their reasoning from environment dynamics, resulting in actions that do not take domain-specific knowledge into account; and (3) LLMs are not designed to learn from visual interactions, which makes it harder for them to make better policies for specific domains. In this paper, we introduce EMAC+, an Embodied Multimodal Agent that collaboratively integrates LLM and VLM via a bidirectional training paradigm. Unlike existing methods, EMAC+ dynamically refines high-level textual plans generated by an LLM using real-time feedback from a VLM executing low-level visual control tasks. We address critical limitations of previous models by enabling the LLM to internalize visual environment dynamics directly through interactive experience, rather than relying solely on static symbolic mappings. Extensive experimental evaluations on ALFWorld and RT-1 benchmarks demonstrate that EMAC+ achieves superior task performance, robustness against noisy observations, and efficient learning. We also conduct thorough ablation studies and provide detailed analyses of success and failure cases.

organization_info.name: University of New South Wales Sydney

keyInsights: A closed-loop co-adaptive framework between an LLM (for high-level planning) and a VLM (for low-level execution and visual feedback) allows the LLM to internalize environment dynamics and domain-specific knowledge.
Dynamic replanning and plan modification by the LLM, based on real-time visual feedback, are crucial for adapting to environmental changes and generating practical, feasible actions.

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) loss is more effective than token-level cross-entropy loss for aligning models with expert preferences in discrete language spaces, as it reinforces complete action sequences and their causal links.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are text-centric, limiting their direct application in physical environments due to an inability to process or learn from visual inputs.
Existing multimodal agents often use LLMs as static planners, generating symbolic instructions without accounting for dynamic environmental feedback, kinematic constraints, or sensor noise.

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) frequently rely on static image-text pair alignments, hindering their capacity to understand dynamic visual environments or translate abstract plans into precise low-level control actions.
results: EMAC+ achieved higher success rates and required fewer average interaction steps on the ALFWorld benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art LLM agents like AutoGen and DEPS.
The framework demonstrated enhanced robustness against noisy visual and textual observations, showing greater stability compared to LLM-only agents and other VLM agents.

On the RT-1 robotics platform, EMAC+ significantly outperformed PaLM-E in planning tasks and surpassed PaLM-E-12B in long-horizon few-shot planning tasks, indicating improved generalization and synchronization of textual and visual states.
solution: EMAC+ establishes a collaborative, bidirectional training paradigm where an LLM expert dynamically refines high-level textual plans based on real-time visual execution feedback from a VLM agent.
It utilizes a modularized VLM to process pixel observations and task instructions for generating low-level control actions, while a parallel 'text world' provides symbolic guidance.

A retrospective feedback loop allows the LLM to build short-term and long-term memory from VLM-environment interactions, learning environment dynamics and adapting planned action sequences accordingly.
paper_summary.summary: EMAC+ introduces an embodied multimodal agent that facilitates collaborative planning between an LLM and a VLM, enabling the LLM to dynamically refine high-level textual plans based on real-time visual feedback. This framework demonstrates superior task performance, enhanced robustness against noisy observations, and improved learning efficiency on both ALFWorld and RT-1 benchmarks.

title: EMAC+: Embodied Multimodal Agent for Collaborative Planning with VLM+LLM

abstract:
Enabling humanoid robots to perform long-horizon mobile manipulation planning
in real-world environments based on embodied perception and comprehension
abilities has been a longstanding challenge. With the recent rise of large
language models (LLMs), there has been a notable increase in the development of
LLM-based planners. These approaches either utilize human-provided textual
representations of the real world or heavily depend on prompt engineering to
extract such representations, lacking the capability to quantitatively
understand the environment, such as determining the feasibility of manipulating
objects. To address these limitations, we present the Instruction-Augmented
Long-Horizon Planning (IALP) system, a novel framework that employs LLMs to
generate feasible and optimal actions based on real-time sensor feedback,
including grounded knowledge of the environment, in a closed-loop interaction.
Distinct from prior works, our approach augments user instructions into PDDL
problems by leveraging both the abstract reasoning capabilities of LLMs and
grounding mechanisms. By conducting various real-world long-horizon tasks, each
consisting of seven distinct manipulatory skills, our results demonstrate that
the IALP system can efficiently solve these tasks with an average success rate
exceeding 80%. Our proposed method can operate as a high-level planner,
equipping robots with substantial autonomy in unstructured environments through
the utilization of multi-modal sensor inputs.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
Ningbo Institute of Digital Twin, Eastern Institute of Technology

keyInsights: Real-world grounding through sensor feedback is essential for robust robot task execution
Closed-loop replanning enables recovery from failures and adaptation to dynamic environments
Both feasibility assessment and optimal action selection contribute significantly to task success
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based robot planners lack sufficient grounding in real-world environments
Translating high-level instructions into feasible robot actions remains challenging, especially for long-horizon tasks
results: Achieved >80% success rate on complex tasks requiring seven distinct manipulation skills
Successfully demonstrated system performance in unstructured real-world environments
Proved effectiveness through ablation studies showing importance of both feedback and action selection
Enabled natural language instruction processing while maintaining physical feasibility constraints
solution: Developed a closed-loop planning system that augments instructions with environment-based predicates
Integrates LLM reasoning with real-time sensor feedback for continuous plan adaptation
Combines promptable predicates (for abstract reasoning) with grounding mechanisms (for geometric feasibility)
paper_summary.summary: Hong Kong Polytechnic University researchers introduce IALP (Instruction-Augmented Long-Horizon Planning), a novel framework that enables humanoid robots to execute complex manipulation tasks by combining LLM-based planning with real-world grounding mechanisms, achieving over 80% success rate on multi-step mobile manipulation tasks in unstructured environments.

title: Instruction-Augmented Long-Horizon Planning: Embedding Grounding  Mechanisms in Embodied Mobile Manipulation

abstract: Although Vision-Language Models (VLM) have demonstrated impressive planning and reasoning capabilities, translating these abilities into the physical world introduces significant challenges. Conventional Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, which integrate reasoning and action into a monolithic architecture, generalize poorly because they are constrained by scarce, narrow-domain data. While recent dual-system approaches attempt to decouple "thinking" from "acting", they are often constrained by semantic ambiguities within the action module. This ambiguity makes large-scale, cross-task training infeasible. Consequently, these systems typically necessitate fine-tuning on newly collected data when deployed to novel environments, and the cooperation mechanism between the two systems remains ill-defined. To address these limitations, we introduce, for the first time, a framework centered around a generalizable action expert. Our approach utilizes sparse 3D trajectories as an intermediate representation, effectively bridging the high-level planning capabilities of the VLM with the low-level physical action module. During the planning phase, the VLM is only required to generate coarse 3D waypoints. These waypoints are then processed by our generalizable action expert, which refines them into dense, executable action sequences by sampling real-time point cloud observations of the environment. To promote training efficiency and robust generalization, we introduce a novel "Action Pre-training, Pointcloud Fine-tuning" paradigm. Our method combines the broad generalization capabilities of VLMs in visual understanding and planning with the fine-grained, action-level generalization of action expert.

keyInsights: True decoupling between VLM planning and action execution is achieved by using sparse 3D trajectories, allowing the VLM to focus on high-level reasoning and the action expert on geometric refinement.
The APPF training paradigm significantly boosts training efficiency and generalization by mastering basic motion skills independently before integrating environmental awareness.
Introducing noise to goal poses during action expert training is crucial for robustness, enabling the expert to generalize to the inherent variability of VLM-generated trajectories.
originalProblem: Monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models suffer from catastrophic forgetting when fine-tuned for precise robotic control, losing their broad VLM knowledge and limiting generalization.

Dual-system frameworks, while attempting to decouple 'thinking' and 'acting', still rely on semantically ambiguous intermediate representations, preventing large-scale, cross-task training for the action module and hindering generalization.
Existing robotic systems often require extensive fine-tuning for new tasks, visual domains, or camera viewpoints, limiting zero-shot deployment capabilities.
results: The model achieved a 60% average success rate on long-horizon tasks in the RoboTwin benchmark, outperforming other generalist models and matching single-task experts on shorter tasks.
It demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization across novel camera viewpoints, objects, colors, and semantics, maintaining minimal performance drop on out-of-domain perspectives.
In real-world experiments on a Franka Research 3 arm, the VLM+DP (PromptDepth) variant achieved the highest average success rate of 0.783 across six diverse tasks, validating its practical viability.
solution: A dual-system framework is proposed, explicitly decoupling the VLM (Thinking Module) from a Generalizable Action Expert (Acting Module) via sparse sequences of coarse 3D waypoints as an unambiguous interface.

The VLM predicts 3D waypoints and end-effector poses directly in the camera frame, aligning with its vision-centric pre-training, and these sparse points are B-spline interpolated into dense trajectories.

A novel 'Action Pre-training, Pointcloud Fine-tuning' (APPF) paradigm is introduced for the action expert, first pre-training on pure trajectory data with masked point clouds for motor skill acquisition, then fine-tuning with full point cloud conditioning for environment-aware refinement.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University and Shanghai AI Lab introduce a framework that decouples Vision-Language Model planning from robotic action execution, using sparse 3D trajectories as an explicit interface. This approach enables a generalizable action expert to achieve zero-shot deployment across diverse tasks and environments, demonstrating superior performance on simulation benchmarks and a 0.783 average success rate in real-world manipulation tasks.

title: Bridge Thinking and Acting: Unleashing Physical Potential of VLM with Generalizable Action Expert

abstract: Operating robots in open-ended scenarios with diverse tasks is a crucial research and application direction in robotics. While recent progress in natural language processing and large multimodal models has enhanced robots' ability to understand complex instructions, robot manipulation still faces the procedural skill dilemma and the declarative skill dilemma in open environments. Existing methods often compromise cognitive and executive capabilities. To address these challenges, in this paper, we propose RoBridge, a hierarchical intelligent architecture for general robotic manipulation. It consists of a high-level cognitive planner (HCP) based on a large-scale pre-trained vision-language model (VLM), an invariant operable representation (IOR) serving as a symbolic bridge, and a generalist embodied agent (GEA). RoBridge maintains the declarative skill of VLM and unleashes the procedural skill of reinforcement learning, effectively bridging the gap between cognition and execution. RoBridge demonstrates significant performance improvements over existing baselines, achieving a 75% success rate on new tasks and an 83% average success rate in sim-to-real generalization using only five real-world data samples per task. This work represents a significant step towards integrating cognitive reasoning with physical execution in robotic systems, offering a new paradigm for general robotic manipulation.

keyInsights: An Invariant Operable Representation (IOR) can effectively bridge abstract cognitive planning and robust physical execution, inspired by Central Pattern Generators in human motor control.
Combining powerful Vision-Language Models for high-level reasoning with reinforcement learning for robust execution allows each component to operate without mutual constraints.

Extensive domain randomization during RL training, followed by imitation learning with aggressive data augmentation and an adaptive DAgger mechanism, is critical for strong sim-to-real transfer and zero-shot generalization.
originalProblem: Data-driven embodied models lack robustness to environmental variations, while reinforcement learning for robotics is often inefficient in real-world scenarios.
Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at high-level planning but often produce physically implausible action sequences due to a lack of embodied experience.
There is a persistent disconnection between a robot's cognitive abstraction (understanding/planning) and its physical execution (low-level control).
results: RoBridge achieved an 82.12% average success rate on the MetaWorld benchmark, outperforming the best baseline by 11.28%, and demonstrated 100% success on Robosuite contact-rich tasks.
The system showed strong real-world generalization with an 83.3% average success rate across diverse tasks, requiring only five real-world data samples per task for fine-tuning.
RoBridge achieved an impressive 75% average success rate on five entirely novel, unseen manipulation tasks, showcasing zero-shot generalization capabilities.
solution: RoBridge proposes a hierarchical architecture with a High-level Cognitive Planner (HCP), an Invariant Operable Representation (IOR), and a Guided Embodied Agent (GEA).
The HCP utilizes large VLMs with vision APIs for task decomposition into primitive actions and generates initial IORs.

The IOR serves as a symbolic, physically intuitive, and environmentally invariant bridge, using masked depth images and constraints, which the GEA translates into low-level robotic actions via trained policies and closed-loop control.
paper_summary.summary: A hierarchical architecture named RoBridge effectively unifies large Vision-Language Models with reinforcement learning via an Invariant Operable Representation, achieving 82.12% average success on MetaWorld and 75% zero-shot generalization to unseen robotic manipulation tasks with minimal real-world data.

title: RoBridge: A Hierarchical Architecture Bridging Cognition and Execution for General Robotic Manipulation

abstract: Robotic foundation models achieve strong generalization by leveraging internet-scale vision-language representations, but their massive computational cost creates a fundamental bottleneck: high inference latency. In dynamic environments, this latency breaks the control loop, rendering powerful models unsafe for real-time deployment. We propose AsyncVLA, an asynchronous control framework that decouples semantic reasoning from reactive execution. Inspired by hierarchical control, AsyncVLA runs a large foundation model on a remote workstation to provide high-level guidance, while a lightweight, onboard Edge Adapter continuously refines actions at high frequency. To bridge the domain gap between these asynchronous streams, we introduce an end-to-end finetuning protocol and a trajectory re-weighting strategy that prioritizes dynamic interactions. We evaluate our approach on real-world vision-based navigation tasks with communication delays up to 6 seconds. AsyncVLA achieves a 40% higher success rate than state-of-the-art baselines, effectively bridging the gap between the semantic intelligence of large models and the reactivity required for edge robotics.

keyInsights: Decoupling high-level semantic reasoning from rapid, reactive action execution allows large, computationally intensive foundation models to be practically deployed on resource-constrained edge robots.
A lightweight onboard adapter can effectively leverage delayed high-level semantic guidance while maintaining real-time reactivity to immediate environmental changes and dynamic obstacles.

End-to-end training and targeted data re-balancing are critical for harmonizing the asynchronous streams and enabling robust performance in the presence of significant and variable communication delays.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, while powerful for generalization, incur high inference latency, making them impractical for real-time control on robots.
Mobile robots typically have limited onboard computing resources, necessitating remote execution of VLAs, which introduces additional, often variable, communication delays.
These accumulated delays lead to stale observations and delayed actions, preventing robots from reacting swiftly to dynamic obstacles in their environment and causing safety concerns.
results: AsyncVLA achieved an 85% success rate in real-world mobile robot navigation with dynamic obstacles, outperforming state-of-the-art synchronous baselines by 40% in success rate and significantly reducing navigation time.

The framework demonstrated robust operation even under artificially enforced communication latencies of up to 5 seconds, maintaining a 50% success rate in dynamic environments where synchronous baselines failed completely.

It successfully preserved the rich language and visual understanding of the large Base VLA for language-conditioned navigation while simultaneously providing the rapid, reactive obstacle avoidance necessary for dynamic environments.
solution: AsyncVLA implements an asynchronous dual-loop control architecture, where a slow, remote Base VLA provides high-level semantic guidance and a fast, lightweight Edge Adapter operates on the robot for reactive action refinement.

The Edge Adapter continuously integrates current onboard visual observations with delayed action token embeddings from the remote Base VLA to generate immediate actions, thereby compensating for latency.

A specialized training protocol, including up-weighting of reactive trajectories and end-to-end fine-tuning of both the Base VLA and Edge Adapter, aligns the asynchronous components to handle dynamic changes and delays.
paper_summary.summary: AsyncVLA is an asynchronous control framework that enables large Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models to run on edge robots, decoupling semantic reasoning from reactive execution. This system achieved an 85% success rate in real-world dynamic navigation tasks, representing a 40% improvement over synchronous baselines, even when faced with communication latencies of up to 5 seconds.

title: AsyncVLA: An Asynchronous VLA for Fast and Robust Navigation on the Edge

abstract:
Recent advancements in open-world robot manipulation have been largely driven
by vision-language models (VLMs). While these models exhibit strong
generalization ability in high-level planning, they struggle to predict
low-level robot controls due to limited physical-world understanding. To
address this issue, we propose a model predictive control framework for
open-world manipulation that combines the semantic reasoning capabilities of
VLMs with physically-grounded, interactive digital twins of the real-world
environments. By constructing and simulating the digital twins, our approach
generates feasible motion trajectories, simulates corresponding outcomes, and
prompts the VLM with future observations to evaluate and select the most
suitable outcome based on language instructions of the task. To further enhance
the capability of pre-trained VLMs in understanding complex scenes for robotic
control, we leverage the flexible rendering capabilities of the digital twin to
synthesize the scene at various novel, unoccluded viewpoints. We validate our
approach on a diverse set of complex manipulation tasks, demonstrating superior
performance compared to baseline methods for language-conditioned robotic
control using VLMs.

keyInsights: Decoupling semantic understanding (VLM) from explicit physical reasoning, which is handled by an interactive digital twin, allows VLMs to operate in-distribution by evaluating predicted future observations.
Providing VLMs with simulated future outcomes as visual prompts, generated by a physics-aware digital twin, enables robust evaluation of candidate actions for robot control.
Adaptive multi-view rendering and task decomposition into subgoals enhance the VLM's spatial reasoning and improve planning robustness for complex, multi-stage manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with precise, low-level robotic control due to their inherent lack of fine-grained physical insight and implicit reasoning capabilities for dynamics and contact.
Existing VLM-based robotic control methods often generalize poorly to novel environments and unseen tasks, requiring extensive robot demonstration data or failing in complex, open-world scenarios.
Traditional Model Predictive Control (MPC) methods typically rely on manually designed task-specific cost functions, limiting their adaptability to open-world, high-level language instructions.
results: Achieved consistently higher success rates across eight diverse real-world manipulation tasks compared to state-of-the-art open-vocabulary VLM-based methods (VoxPoser*, MOKA) and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models (OpenVLA, π0).
Ablation studies confirmed that adaptive multi-view observations, subgoal decomposition, and the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) are crucial components, each significantly contributing to performance.
Qualitative results demonstrated accurate physical simulation, effective VLM evaluation of simulated outcomes, and strong alignment between digital twin predictions and real-world robot trajectories.
solution: Introduces "Prompting with the Future" (PWTF), an MPC framework that integrates Vision-Language Models with explicit physics modeling provided by an interactive digital twin.

Automatically constructs a high-fidelity, interactive digital twin of the real-world workspace from a quick handheld video scan, using a hybrid 3D representation of Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering and meshes for accurate physical simulation.

Leverages the VLM as a 'critic' within a sampling-based planner (Cross-Entropy Method), where the VLM evaluates multi-view renderings of diverse simulated future outcomes against high-level language instructions to select optimal robot actions.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents "Prompting with the Future" (PWTF), a Model Predictive Control framework that enables open-world robotic manipulation by using interactive digital twins to provide Vision-Language Models (VLMs) with explicit physical foresight. The system consistently achieved higher success rates on eight diverse real-world tasks compared to existing VLM-based and VLA robot control methods, without requiring task-specific robot demonstration data or VLM fine-tuning.

title: Prompting with the Future: Open-World Model Predictive Control with  Interactive Digital Twins

abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models and LLM agents have advanced rapidly, yet reliable deployment on physical robots is often hindered by an interface mismatch between agent tool APIs and robot middleware. Current implementations typically rely on ad-hoc wrappers that are difficult to reuse, and changes to the VLA backend or serving stack often necessitate extensive re-integration. We introduce RoboNeuron, a middleware layer that connects the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for LLM agents with robot middleware such as ROS2. RoboNeuron bridges these ecosystems by deriving agent-callable tools directly from ROS schemas, providing a unified execution abstraction that supports both direct commands and modular composition, and localizing backend, runtime, and acceleration-preset changes within a stable inference boundary. We evaluate RoboNeuron in simulation and on hardware through multi-platform base control, arm motion, and VLA-based grasping tasks, demonstrating that it enables modular system orchestration under a unified interface while supporting backend transitions without system rewiring. The full code implementation of this work is available at github repo: this https URL
title: RoboNeuron: A Middle-Layer Infrastructure for Agent-Driven Orchestration in Embodied AI

abstract: Task planning for robotic manipulation with large language models (LLMs) is an emerging area. Prior approaches rely on specialized models, fine tuning, or prompt tuning, and often operate in an open loop manner without robust environmental feedback, making them fragile in dynamic settings. MALLVI presents a Multi Agent Large Language and Vision framework that enables closed-loop feedback driven robotic manipulation. Given a natural language instruction and an image of the environment, MALLVI generates executable atomic actions for a robot manipulator. After action execution, a Vision Language Model (VLM) evaluates environmental feedback and decides whether to repeat the process or proceed to the next step. Rather than using a single model, MALLVI coordinates specialized agents, Decomposer, Localizer, Thinker, and Reflector, to manage perception, localization, reasoning, and high level planning. An optional Descriptor agent provides visual memory of the initial state. The Reflector supports targeted error detection and recovery by reactivating only relevant agents, avoiding full replanning. Experiments in simulation and real-world settings show that iterative closed loop multi agent coordination improves generalization and increases success rates in zero shot manipulation tasks. Code available at this https URL .

keyInsights: Distributing robotic manipulation responsibilities among specialized LLM agents in a multi-agent framework significantly enhances robustness, modularity, and reduces planning bottlenecks compared to monolithic approaches.

A dedicated 'Reflector' agent for real-time visual verification and targeted error recovery is crucial for enabling self-correction and achieving higher task success rates in dynamic environments, avoiding costly global replanning.

The parallel processing of environmental description and task decomposition, combined with robust object localization and 3D projection, improves the grounding of abstract instructions into actionable robot commands.
originalProblem: Bridging the abstraction gap between high-level natural language instructions and concrete, feedback-driven robotic actions in dynamic, unstructured environments.
Addressing the fragility of open-loop LLM-based planning systems, which are prone to accumulating errors and generating unfeasible plans without continuous environmental feedback.
Overcoming the limitations of monolithic single-model systems for robotic control, which can create bottlenecks, limit specialization, and hinder robust error recovery.
results: MALLVi achieved the highest success rates across eight real-world manipulation tasks, including 100% for 'Place Food' and 95% for 'Put Shape', outperforming existing methods like MALMM and VoxPoser.
The framework demonstrated superior performance on 12 VIMABench tasks (e.g., 100% in 'Simple Manipulation', 95% in 'Novel Concepts') and nine RLBench tasks (e.g., 92% for 'Put in Safe').

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the multi-agent design and the Reflector agent, with single-agent and 'w/o Reflector' configurations showing significant performance drops (e.g., a 75% reduction for 'Place Food' in the single-agent baseline).
solution: A multi-agent architecture (MALLVi) comprising specialized LLM agents (Decomposer, Descriptor, Localizer, Thinker, Actor, Reflector) that communicate through shared object and memory tags.
A self-correcting, closed-loop pipeline where a Reflector agent provides real-time visual feedback, verifies subtask success, and selectively reactivates specific failing agents for retry.
Hierarchical task decomposition and robust visual grounding, which translates high-level instructions into executable atomic subtasks with accurate object identification and 3D localization.
paper_summary.summary: The MALLVi framework introduces a multi-agent architecture for generalized robotics manipulation, enabling robots to interpret natural language instructions and execute tasks reliably in dynamic environments. It utilizes specialized LLM agents for planning, perception, and real-time verification, demonstrating higher success rates across various simulated and real-world manipulation tasks in zero-shot settings.

title: MALLVI: A Multi-Agent Framework for Integrated Generalized Robotics Manipulation

abstract: Bridging the gap between natural language commands and autonomous execution in unstructured environments remains an open challenge for robotics. This requires robots to perceive and reason over the current task scene through multiple modalities, and to plan their behaviors to achieve their intended goals. Traditional robotic task-planning approaches often struggle to bridge low-level execution with high-level task reasoning, and cannot dynamically update task strategies when instructions change during execution, which ultimately limits their versatility and adaptability to new tasks. In this work, we propose a novel language model-based framework for dynamic robot task planning. Our Vision-Language-Policy (VLP) model, based on a vision-language model fine-tuned on real-world data, can interpret semantic instructions and integrate reasoning over the current task scene to generate behavior policies that control the robot to accomplish the task. Moreover, it can dynamically adjust the task strategy in response to changes in the task, enabling flexible adaptation to evolving task requirements. Experiments conducted with different robots and a variety of real-world tasks show that the trained model can efficiently adapt to novel scenarios and dynamically update its policy, demonstrating strong planning autonomy and cross-embodiment generalization. Videos: this https URL

keyInsights: Fine-tuning a VLM with a relatively modest, robot-specific dataset via LoRA is effective for robust task planning and dynamic adaptation capabilities.
Generating structured, interpretable JSON-formatted policies from a VLM allows for clear reasoning and real-time adjustment, addressing the opaqueness of end-to-end VLA systems.

Utilizing a library of abstract action and perception primitives enables the VLP model to generalize effectively across different robot embodiments without requiring specific retraining for each platform.
originalProblem: Traditional robotic planning methods lack dynamic adaptability to changing instructions or environments, hindering versatility in unstructured settings.
A significant gap exists between high-level semantic understanding from natural language and the precise low-level physical control required for robot execution.
Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often require extensive datasets, lack interpretability, and struggle with efficient local deployment on resource-constrained robotic platforms.
