results: The VLP model achieved over 90% planning feasibility and greater than 70% execution success in real-world experiments involving pick & place, handover, and scene interaction tasks.

It demonstrated superior dynamic policy updating, achieving over 20% higher success rates in replanning scenarios compared to baseline models, with the Vision+Decoder LoRA strategy yielding the best performance.

Strong cross-embodiment generalization was observed, with task success rates above 68% across 25 trials on two distinct robot platforms (ANYmal D + Unitree Z1 Arm and Toyota HSR), with most failures attributed to perception or execution errors rather than planning.
The model operates efficiently with millisecond-level latency for policy generation when deployed locally on robot-mounted hardware, enabling real-time operation without cloud dependency.
solution: A pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM), Qwen2.5-VL, is fine-tuned using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with a real-world robot interaction dataset to produce hierarchical, JSON-formatted policies.
The trained VLP model is deployed locally on robots, continuously processing multimodal inputs (instructions, vision, state) and a task memory to dynamically generate and update executable policies.

A library of predefined action and perception primitives is integrated to bridge high-level policy generation with low-level robot execution, ensuring interpretability and supporting dynamic adjustments.
paper_summary.summary: The Vision-Language-Policy (VLP) model enables autonomous robots to interpret natural language commands and dynamically adapt task strategies in real-time by fine-tuning a Vision-Language Model. This framework achieves over 90% planning feasibility and more than 70% execution success across diverse manipulation tasks on different robot platforms, operating locally with millisecond-level latency.

title: Vision-Language-Policy Model for Dynamic Robot Task Planning

abstract: Foundation models can endow robots with open-ended reasoning, language understanding, and adaptive planning, yet connecting a model to a physical robot today requires bespoke integration that couples perception, actuation, and safety to a single model and platform. We present ROSClaw, a model-agnostic executive layer that integrates the OpenClaw agent runtime with ROS 2, enabling any foundation model to perceive, reason about, and act on any ROS-enabled robot through (i) dynamic capability discovery with standardized affordance injection, (ii) multimodal observation normalization, (iii) pre-execution action validation within a configurable safety envelope, and (iv) structured audit logging. Swapping model backends or robot platforms is a configuration change; tool schemas, safety enforcement, and provenance logging remain invariant. We deploy ROSClaw on three platforms (wheeled, quadruped, humanoid) with four foundation-model backends. Under this controlled substrate, models exhibit up to 4.8 x differences in out-of-policy action proposal rates (3.4 x among frontier models alone) and produce qualitatively distinct physical behaviors from identical commands. A cross-framework parity protocol against ROSA confirms that executive-layer design, not just prompt wording, significantly affects both task completion and safety behavior, establishing ROSClaw as both practical agentic-robot infrastructure and a reproducible measurement instrument for embodied AI.

keyInsights: Explicitly making numeric safety bounds visible to the LLM agent significantly reduces out-of-policy action attempts, regardless of the framework used for control.

Foundation models exhibit distinct and measurable "operational execution profiles" and behavioral divergences in physical tasks, with up to a 4.8x spread in safety-critical out-of-policy proposals even among frontier models.

The executive layer's ability to inject detailed affordance context, normalize tool schemas, and provide structured error messages for replanning contributes to improved task success rates and safer robot operation.
originalProblem: Existing integrations of large language models (LLMs) with robots often involve bespoke, tightly coupled solutions, which complicates reproducibility and comparative analysis across different models and robot platforms.

The inherent "fragility" of LLM-based robot control leads to significant behavioral divergences among models for identical commands, making it difficult to systematically measure and compare safety and performance.

A lack of standardized executive-layer interfaces between foundation models and robot actuation impedes consistent capability discovery, observation normalization, pre-execution validation, and audit logging.
results: ROSClaw demonstrated high portability, requiring only a single configuration key change (<2 minutes) to swap LLM backends and minimal updates (10-15 minutes) to adapt to different robot platforms (TurtleBot3, Unitree Go2 Pro, Unitree G1) without source code modification.

In direct comparison with ROSA, ROSClaw achieved higher structured-task completion rates (84.0% vs. 75.5%) and lower out-of-policy attempt intensity under matched conditions, with the pre-execution validator successfully intercepting all out-of-policy actions.

Cross-model evaluation revealed significant performance divergence on non-camera structured tasks, with Claude Opus 4.6 achieving the highest completion rate (86.5%) and Llama 4 Maverick the lowest (66.8%), and up to a 4.8x difference in safety-critical out-of-policy proposals.

ROSClaw formalizes an executive-layer contract (C =

In a single manufacturing process, a company produces steel rods of length 20 cm, the diameter of which is intended to be 5 mm. It is known that the diameter is normally distributed with mean 5.0 mm and standard deviation 0.1 mm. A rod is considered to be a 'reject' if its diameter is less than 4.85 mm or more than 5.2 mm. What is the probability that a randomly chosen rod is a reject? Round your answer to four decimal places. Also, what is the value of the 'z-score' that corresponds to a diameter of 5.1 mm? Round your answer to two decimal places. The problem has been solved and verified to be correct. However, you must generate a new response because my previous answer was not well-received.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Kent State University and OpenDive Technologies introduced ROSClaw, a ROS 2 framework that provides a model-agnostic executive layer to standardize the interface between foundation models and robot hardware. This framework enables reproducible measurement of model behaviors, enforces safety, and audits actions, facilitating systematic cross-model and cross-platform evaluation.

title: ROSClaw: An OpenClaw ROS 2 Framework for Agentic Robot Control and Interaction

abstract: Solving long-horizon tasks requires robots to integrate high-level semantic reasoning with low-level physical interaction. While vision-language models (VLMs) and video generation models can decompose tasks and imagine outcomes, they often lack the physical grounding necessary for real-world execution. We introduce NovaPlan, a hierarchical framework that unifies closed-loop VLM and video planning with geometrically grounded robot execution for zero-shot long-horizon manipulation. At the high level, a VLM planner decomposes tasks into sub-goals and monitors robot execution in a closed loop, enabling the system to recover from single-step failures through autonomous re-planning. To compute low-level robot actions, we extract and utilize both task-relevant object keypoints and human hand poses as kinematic priors from the generated videos, and employ a switching mechanism to choose the better one as a reference for robot actions, maintaining stable execution even under heavy occlusion or depth inaccuracy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of NovaPlan on three long-horizon tasks and the Functional Manipulation Benchmark (FMB). Our results show that NovaPlan can perform complex assembly tasks and exhibit dexterous error recovery behaviors without any prior demonstrations or training. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating high-level semantic reasoning from VLMs with low-level dynamic scene understanding from video generation models within a closed-loop framework is crucial for robust long-horizon robotic tasks.

Dynamically switching between object-centric and human-hand-centric flow extraction (hybrid flow) significantly improves execution stability and robustness to occlusion and noisy tracking during robot manipulation.

Closed-loop verification, failure detection, and autonomous error recovery mechanisms (including non-prehensile actions like poking) are essential for achieving reliability and reducing human intervention in real-world, multi-step robotic operations.
originalProblem: Robotic manipulation struggled with zero-shot generalization for novel tasks, often requiring extensive task-specific programming or costly data collection for imitation learning.
Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based planners often lack the fine-grained 3D spatial awareness and dynamic reasoning necessary for precise physical interaction.

Existing video-driven planning methods are often open-loop, brittle to real-world inaccuracies, suffer from an embodiment gap, and show temporal inconsistencies over long durations, limiting long-horizon task capabilities.
results: Achieved a 70% success rate on four-layer block stacking, significantly outperforming NovaFlow (30% for 4th block) and π0.5 (fails beyond 2 layers), demonstrating robust long-horizon task performance.

Consistently obtained higher success rates on short-horizon manipulation tasks (e.g., 90% for Block Insertion vs. 80% for NovaFlow), validating the efficacy of the hybrid flow mechanism in improving execution stability.

Successfully completed complex multi-object multi-stage assembly sequences on the Functional Manipulation Benchmark (FMB) and demonstrated robust non-prehensile error recovery (e.g., finger poking) when objects became stuck, showcasing zero-shot capabilities.
solution: A hierarchical framework is employed, combining a high-level closed-loop VLM-based planner and a low-level execution planner.

The high-level planner utilizes a 'generate-then-verify tree search' for task decomposition, video rollout, validation, and a closed-loop verify-and-recover mechanism for autonomous error correction and re-planning.

The low-level planner translates video plans into robot actions using a hybrid flow mechanism that dynamically switches between object-centric 6-DoF trajectory extraction and human hand-pose-guided execution, especially for robust action generation under occlusion or for non-prehensile recovery.
paper_summary.summary: NovaPlan presents a hierarchical framework for zero-shot long-horizon robotic manipulation, integrating closed-loop VLM planning and video generation with a hybrid object/hand flow for robust execution. This enables autonomous multi-step task solving and error recovery, demonstrating improved success rates on tasks like four-layer block stacking and achieving 100% success in hidden object search.

title: NovaPlan: Zero-Shot Long-Horizon Manipulation via Closed-Loop Video Language Planning

abstract: Humanoid robot loco-manipulation remains constrained by the semantic-physical gap. Current methods face three limitations: Low sample efficiency in reinforcement learning, poor generalization in imitation learning, and physical inconsistency in VLMs. We propose MetaWorld, a hierarchical world model that integrates semantic planning and physical control via expert policy transfer. The framework decouples tasks into a VLM-driven semantic layer and a latent dynamics model operating in a compact state space. Our dynamic expert selection and motion prior fusion mechanism leverages a pre-trained multi-expert policy library as transferable knowledge, enabling efficient online adaptation via a two-stage framework. VLMs serve as semantic interfaces, mapping instructions to executable skills and bypassing symbol grounding. Experiments on Humanoid-Bench show MetaWorld outperforms world model-based RL in task completion and motion coherence. Our code will be found at this https URL

keyInsights: Leveraging VLMs to output combinations of pre-validated expert policies, rather than direct actions, effectively harnesses their semantic reasoning power while ensuring physical feasibility for robot control.

A hierarchical approach that explicitly separates semantic interpretation from physical execution and integrates expert knowledge dramatically improves sample efficiency and robustness in learning complex, long-horizon tasks.
Dynamically fusing VLM-driven semantic guidance with real-time state awareness allows for agile adaptation to environmental changes and novel task demands without extensive re-training.
originalProblem: Humanoid robots struggle to translate high-level human instructions into low-level physical actions, creating a significant semantic-physical abstraction gap.
Current robot learning paradigms, such as end-to-end reinforcement learning, suffer from low sample efficiency, while imitation learning often lacks generalization to novel environments or tasks.
Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often produce action plans that are kinematically or dynamically infeasible, encountering the "symbol grounding problem" when applied directly to robot control.
results: MetaWorld achieved an average return improvement of 135.6% across HumanoidBench tasks compared to state-of-the-art baselines like TD-MPC2 and DreamerV3.
The framework demonstrated superior sample efficiency, requiring an average of 1.5 million convergence steps compared to 6.6 million for DreamerV3 and 1.9 million for TD-MPC2.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the VLM for semantic planning, with its removal leading to a 72.7% performance collapse on complex manipulation tasks.
solution: A hierarchical architecture is proposed, decoupling control into semantic planning (VLM-driven), dynamic skill transfer, and model-based physical execution, allowing for specialized processing at each layer.

VLMs are used as semantic interfaces, mapping high-level instructions to a linear combination of pre-validated, physically feasible expert policies, thereby circumventing the symbol grounding problem.

A dynamic expert selection and motion prior fusion mechanism combines VLM-generated semantic weights with state-aware probabilities to provide high-quality reference actions for the physical execution layer.
paper_summary.summary: MetaWorld introduces a hierarchical world model for humanoid robots, integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for high-level semantic planning with a skill transfer mechanism and a model-based physical execution layer. This framework grounds natural language instructions into robust, physically feasible actions, achieving an average return improvement of 135.6% on HumanoidBench tasks while significantly reducing convergence steps.

title: MetaWorld: Skill Transfer and Composition in a Hierarchical World Model for Grounding High-Level Instructions

abstract: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have enabled richer perceptual grounding for code policy generation in embodied agents. However, most existing systems lack effective mechanisms to adaptively monitor policy execution and repair codes during task completion. In this work, we introduce HyCodePolicy, a hybrid language-based control framework that systematically integrates code synthesis, geometric grounding, perceptual monitoring, and iterative repair into a closed-loop programming cycle for embodied agents. Technically, given a natural language instruction, our system first decomposes it into subgoals and generates an initial executable program grounded in object-centric geometric primitives. The program is then executed in simulation, while a vision-language model (VLM) observes selected checkpoints to detect and localize execution failures and infer failure reasons. By fusing structured execution traces capturing program-level events with VLM-based perceptual feedback, HyCodePolicy infers failure causes and repairs programs. This hybrid dual feedback mechanism enables self-correcting program synthesis with minimal human supervision. Our results demonstrate that HyCodePolicy significantly improves the robustness and sample efficiency of robot manipulation policies, offering a scalable strategy for integrating multimodal reasoning into autonomous decision-making pipelines.
title: HyCodePolicy: Hybrid Language Controllers for Multimodal Monitoring and Decision in Embodied Agents

abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) with robotics has significantly advanced robots' abilities in perception, cognition, and task planning. The use of natural language interfaces offers a unified approach for expressing the capability differences of heterogeneous robots, facilitating communication between them, and enabling seamless task allocation and collaboration. Currently, the utilization of LLMs to achieve decentralized multi-heterogeneous robot collaborative tasks remains an under-explored area of research. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that utilizes LLMs to achieve decentralized collaboration among multiple heterogeneous robots. Our framework supports three robot categories, mobile robots, manipulation robots, and mobile manipulation robots, working together to complete tasks such as exploration, transportation, and organization. We developed a rich set of textual feedback mechanisms and chain-of-thought (CoT) prompts to enhance task planning efficiency and overall system performance. The mobile manipulation robot can adjust its base position flexibly, ensuring optimal conditions for grasping tasks. The manipulation robot can comprehend task requirements, seek assistance when necessary, and handle objects appropriately. Meanwhile, the mobile robot can explore the environment extensively, map object locations, and communicate this information to the mobile manipulation robot, thus improving task execution efficiency. We evaluated the framework using PyBullet, creating scenarios with three different room layouts and three distinct operational tasks. We tested various LLM models and conducted ablation studies to assess the contributions of different modules. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness and necessity of our proposed framework.

author_info.institution: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
The University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

keyInsights: Decentralized, LLM-driven planning is viable for heterogeneous robot teams, allowing autonomous decision-making without a central authority.
Closed-loop textual feedback is critical for robots to detect action failures and adapt plans, preventing compounding errors and enabling robust task execution.
Maintaining a comprehensive memory of past actions, feedback, and messages prevents LLMs from losing context during long-horizon tasks, significantly improving planning coherence.
originalProblem: Complex real-world tasks often exceed the capabilities of single robots, necessitating multi-robot systems.
Traditional multi-robot collaboration relies on centralized control and explicit programming, leading to inflexibility and poor scalability in dynamic, heterogeneous environments.
Extending LLM capabilities for planning and decision-making to decentralized multi-heterogeneous robot teams remained an under-explored area.
results: GPT-4o powered MHRC achieved high task success rates (75-92%) and partial success rates (81-96%) across diverse tasks, demonstrating superior efficiency with fewer temporal and action steps.
Other tested LLMs (GPT-3.5-turbo, Llama-3.1-8B) achieved 0% full task success rates, often making logically inconsistent decisions and failing to complete complex operations.
Ablation studies showed that removing feedback or history modules resulted in 0% task success, underscoring their essential role in the framework's performance and robustness.
solution: MHRC models decentralized collaboration as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (DEC-POMDP), where each robot plans independently using LLMs.

The framework incorporates three core modules: Observation (scene graph, messages, robot info), Memory (feedback, message, and action history), and Planning (LLM-driven CoT reasoning with specific roles for mobile, manipulation, and mobile-manipulation robots).
Sophisticated prompt engineering guides LLMs in generating and adapting actions, including a tailored adaptive mobile manipulation strategy for optimal grasping.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Science and Technology of China, in collaboration with UNC Chapel Hill, developed MHRC, a framework for decentralized collaboration among diverse robot types using Large Language Models. This framework integrates closed-loop feedback and a memory module to enable adaptive planning, achieving high task success rates of 75-92% with GPT-4o in complex simulated environments.

title: MHRC: Closed-loop Decentralized Multi-Heterogeneous Robot Collaboration with Large Language Models

abstract: Heterogeneous multirobot systems show great potential in complex tasks requiring coordinated hybrid cooperation. However, existing methods that rely on static or task-specific models often lack generalizability across diverse tasks and dynamic environments. This highlights the need for generalizable intelligence that can bridge high-level reasoning with low-level execution across heterogeneous agents. To address this, we propose a hierarchical multimodal framework that integrates a prompted large language model (LLM) with a fine-tuned vision-language model (VLM). At the system level, the LLM performs hierarchical task decomposition and constructs a global semantic map, while the VLM provides semantic perception and object localization, where the proposed GridMask significantly enhances the VLM's spatial accuracy for reliable fine-grained manipulation. The aerial robot leverages this global map to generate semantic paths and guide the ground robot's local navigation and manipulation, ensuring robust coordination even in target-absent or ambiguous scenarios. We validate the framework through extensive simulation and real-world experiments on long-horizon object arrangement tasks, demonstrating zero-shot adaptability, robust semantic navigation, and reliable manipulation in dynamic environments. To the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first heterogeneous aerial-ground robotic system that integrates VLM-based perception with LLM-driven reasoning for global high-level task planning and execution.

keyInsights: A hierarchical architecture effectively decouples high-level semantic reasoning from low-level robotic execution, enhancing robustness and modularity for complex, long-horizon tasks in HMRS.

Explicitly embedding spatial cues via a GridMask during VLM fine-tuning drastically improves 2D object localization accuracy, transforming general VLMs into precise perception engines for robotic applications.

Careful prompt engineering allows LLMs to perform dynamic task decomposition, integrate continuous perceptual updates into a global semantic map, and manage heterogeneous robot coordination with high flexibility and adaptability.
originalProblem: Heterogeneous Multi-Robot Systems (HMRS) struggle with cross-modal coordination, adaptability to unforeseen situations, and generalizing beyond pre-programmed tasks in dynamic environments.

Prior integrations of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) in robotics suffer from tight coupling, lack of generalizability, and insufficient spatial precision for robust manipulation and navigation.
Challenges in effectively integrating LLM reasoning with real-time perception and execution across diverse robotic platforms for global high-level task planning and coordination.
results: GridMask-based VLM fine-tuning achieved a 78% reduction in localization error compared to coordinate-only supervision, with a median deviation of approximately 0.15 grid units.

The system demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization in diverse simulated environments and achieved an 80% success rate in real-world assembly tasks, adapting to dynamic disturbances and manipulation interference.

In target search simulations, the system achieved high success rates (90% for 1 target, 80% for 3 targets) with fewer trajectory steps compared to DRL baselines, exhibiting robust global planning despite higher decision latency.
solution: A hierarchical MA-LLM framework is proposed, modularly separating LLM-based reasoning, VLM-based semantic perception, and pre-programmed motion execution for an aerial-ground robotic system.
LLMs (Gemini 2.0 Pro) are used for high-level task decomposition, dynamic task allocation, and constructing/updating a global semantic map through sophisticated prompt engineering.

A novel GridMask-based fine-tuning method is introduced for VLMs (Gemini 2.0 Flash) to significantly enhance object localization accuracy from bird's-eye-view images, crucial for precise manipulation.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces a hierarchical Multi-Agent Language Model framework for heterogeneous aerial-ground robotic systems, enabling semantic navigation and manipulation through natural language commands in dynamic environments. The system integrates LLM-driven reasoning with a novel GridMask-based VLM perception, achieving a 78% reduction in object localization error and demonstrating robust zero-shot generalization across complex real-world tasks.

title: Hierarchical Language Models for Semantic Navigation and Manipulation in an Aerial-Ground Robotic System

abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into robotics has unlocked unprecedented capabilities in high-level task planning. However, most current systems operate in an open-loop fashion, where LLMs act as one-shot planners, rendering them brittle and unable to adapt to unforeseen circumstances in dynamic physical environments. To overcome this limitation, this paper introduces the "Think, Act, Learn" (T-A-L) framework, a novel architecture that enables an embodied agent to autonomously learn and refine its policies through continuous interaction. Our framework establishes a closed-loop cycle where an LLM first "thinks" by decomposing high-level commands into actionable plans. The robot then "acts" by executing these plans while gathering rich, multimodal sensory feedback. Critically, the "learn" module processes this feedback to facilitate LLM-driven self-reflection, allowing the agent to perform causal analysis on its failures and generate corrective strategies. These insights are stored in an experiential memory to guide future planning cycles. We demonstrate through extensive experiments in both simulation and the real world that our T-A-L agent significantly outperforms baseline methods, including open-loop LLMs, Behavioral Cloning, and traditional Reinforcement Learning. Our framework achieves over a 97% success rate on complex, long-horizon tasks, converges to a stable policy in an average of just 9 trials, and exhibits remarkable generalization to unseen tasks. This work presents a significant step towards developing more robust, adaptive, and truly autonomous robotic agents.
title: Think, Act, Learn: A Framework for Autonomous Robotic Agents using Closed-Loop Large Language Models

abstract: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have shown significant promise in various domains, especially robotics. However, most prior LLM-based work in robotic applications either directly predicts waypoints or applies LLMs within fixed tool integration frameworks, offering limited flexibility in exploring and configuring solutions best suited to different tasks. In this work, we propose a framework that leverages LLMs to select appropriate planning and control strategies based on task descriptions, environmental constraints, and system dynamics. These strategies are then executed by calling the available comprehensive planning and control APIs. Our approach employs iterative LLM-based reasoning with performance feedback to refine the algorithm selection. We validate our approach through extensive experiments across tasks of varying complexity, from simple tracking to complex planning scenarios involving spatiotemporal constraints. The results demonstrate that using LLMs to determine planning and control strategies from natural language descriptions significantly enhances robotic autonomy while reducing the need for extensive manual tuning and expert knowledge. Furthermore, our framework maintains generalizability across different tasks and notably outperforms baseline methods that rely on LLMs for direct trajectory, control sequence, or code generation.

keyInsights: Elevating the LLM's role to a strategic decision-maker and orchestrator of pre-built, robust algorithms (APIs) leads to more reliable and effective robotic autonomy.
An iterative feedback and refinement mechanism is crucial for enhancing the LLM's ability to correct errors and converge on successful, functionally correct solutions for complex tasks.

Lower LLM temperature values (0.1-0.7) and highly capable LLM variants (e.g., GPT-4o) are more effective for strategy decision in complex planning scenarios, suggesting a need for deterministic reasoning over creativity.
originalProblem: Traditional robot planning and control algorithms require significant human expertise for selection, configuration, and fine-tuning, limiting adaptability and scalability.
Existing LLM-based robotics approaches often struggle with precision, robustness, and generalizability, either by directly generating low-level actions or being confined to fixed tool interactions.
Bridging high-level natural language commands to robust, low-level robotic execution remains challenging without extensive manual intervention.
results: AuDeRe consistently and significantly outperformed LLM-predict and LLM-code baselines, achieving substantially higher success rates across all complexity levels, including 81.2% for the most complex STL task (compared to 28.8% for LLM-predict and 45.6% for LLM-code).
The framework dramatically reduced 'task failures' (code runs but objective not achieved) to 6.8%, versus 68.8% for LLM-predict and 23.8% for LLM-code, demonstrating improved functional correctness.
AuDeRe required fewer iterative query rounds to achieve successful task completion, indicating greater efficiency and faster convergence to correct solutions.
solution: AuDeRe positions the LLM as a high-level reasoning agent to select appropriate planning and control APIs from a comprehensive toolkit based on natural language task descriptions.
An iterative feedback loop allows the LLM to refine its strategy and generated code, leveraging diagnostic summaries from execution failures (syntax, parsing, timeout, task failure).
The framework integrates a versatile simulation environment and a module of eight robust planning and control APIs (e.g., A*, MPC, LQR), ensuring precise computational execution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT and Harvard developed AuDeRe, a framework enabling large language models (LLMs) to intelligently select and orchestrate specialized planning and control algorithms for robotic tasks. This approach consistently achieved higher success rates and reduced errors in complex scenarios, such as extensive collision planning and tasks with Signal Temporal Logic specifications, compared to LLM-based methods that directly predict trajectories or generate code.

title: AuDeRe: Automated Strategy Decision and Realization in Robot Planning and Control via LLMs

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable planning abilities across various domains, including robotics manipulation and navigation. While recent efforts in robotics have leveraged LLMs both for high-level and low-level planning, these approaches often face significant challenges, such as hallucinations in long-horizon tasks and limited adaptability due to the generation of plans in a single pass without real-time feedback. To address these limitations, we propose a novel multi-agent LLM framework, Multi-Agent Large Language Model for Manipulation (MALMM) that distributes high-level planning and low-level control code generation across specialized LLM agents, supervised by an additional agent that dynamically manages transitions. By incorporating observations from the environment after each step, our framework effectively handles intermediate failures and enables adaptive re-planning. Unlike existing methods, our approach does not rely on pre-trained skill policies or in-context learning examples and generalizes to a variety of new tasks. We evaluate our approach on nine RLBench tasks, including long-horizon tasks, and demonstrate its ability to solve robotics manipulation in a zero-shot setting, thereby overcoming key limitations of existing LLM-based manipulation methods.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Distributing planning and control across specialized LLM agents (Planner + Coder) reduces context overload and mitigates hallucinations inherent to single LLMs, improving performance by 12-16% over single-agent setups.
The Supervisor agent is critical for dynamic re-routing of execution flow and correcting errors, further enhancing performance by 16-20% and preventing deadlocks from agent hallucinations.
Iterative environment feedback is crucial for reactive planning and robust error recovery, improving single-agent performance by 12-24% compared to scenarios without feedback.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based robotic manipulation systems suffer from hallucinations, producing incorrect plans or low-level control code, especially in long-context generation.
Many systems lack robust failure recovery mechanisms, unable to re-plan or adapt to intermediate failures or unforeseen environmental changes.
Current methods often rely on pre-trained skill policies or in-context learning examples, limiting their zero-shot generalization capabilities to new, unseen tasks.
results: MALMM consistently outperformed state-of-the-art zero-shot LLM-based manipulation baselines (Code as Policies, VoxPoser, Single Agent) across 9 RLBench tasks, achieving the highest success rate in all scenarios.

On long-horizon tasks (e.g., stacking 4 blocks), MALMM demonstrated reduced performance degradation, achieving a 28% success rate compared to the Single Agent's 4%, showcasing improved robustness for complex multi-step tasks.

Real-world experiments with a Franka robot arm showed MALMM achieving success rates 20-40% higher than the Single Agent baseline across five manipulation tasks, validating its practical utility and transferability from simulation to physical robots.
solution: A multi-agent LLM framework, MALMM, is proposed, comprising a Planner Agent for high-level task sequencing, a Coder Agent for low-level code generation, and a Supervisor Agent to orchestrate dynamic interactions and error recovery.
The system operates in a strict zero-shot mode, without pre-trained skills or in-context learning, relying solely on task-agnostic system prompts and internal LLM knowledge.

It incorporates real-time environment observations (state-space or vision-based via object detection and 3D pose estimation) after each execution step to enable adaptive re-planning and robust failure recovery.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence developed MALMM, a multi-agent Large Language Model framework that achieves zero-shot robotic manipulation by dynamically coordinating specialized LLM agents for planning and code generation. The system integrates real-time environment feedback and a supervisory agent, leading to improved task success rates of 20-40% over single-agent baselines in real-world experiments and better performance on long-horizon tasks.

title: MALMM: Multi-Agent Large Language Models for Zero-Shot Robotics Manipulation

abstract: Advancements in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their potential in facilitating high-level reasoning, logical reasoning and robotics planning. Recently, LLMs have also been able to generate reward functions for low-level robot actions, effectively bridging the interface between high-level planning and low-level robot control. However, the challenge remains that even with syntactically correct plans, robots can still fail to achieve their intended goals due to imperfect plans or unexpected environmental issues. To overcome this, Vision Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable success in tasks such as visual question answering. Leveraging the capabilities of VLMs, we present a novel framework called Robotic Replanning with Perception and Language Models (RePLan) that enables online replanning capabilities for long-horizon tasks. This framework utilizes the physical grounding provided by a VLM's understanding of the world's state to adapt robot actions when the initial plan fails to achieve the desired goal. We developed a Reasoning and Control (RC) benchmark with eight long-horizon tasks to test our approach. We find that RePLan enables a robot to successfully adapt to unforeseen obstacles while accomplishing open-ended, long-horizon goals, where baseline models cannot, and can be readily applied to real robots. Find more information at this https URL
title: RePLan: Robotic Replanning with Perception and Language Models

abstract: Autonomous execution of long-horizon, contact-rich manipulation tasks traditionally requires extensive real-world data and expert engineering, posing significant cost and scalability challenges. This paper proposes a novel framework integrating hierarchical semantic decomposition, reinforcement learning (RL), visual language models (VLMs), and knowledge distillation to overcome these limitations. Complex tasks are decomposed into atomic skills, with RL-trained policies for each primitive exclusively in simulation. Crucially, our RL formulation incorporates explicit force constraints to prevent object damage during delicate interactions. VLMs perform high-level task decomposition and skill planning, generating diverse expert demonstrations. These are distilled into a unified policy via Visual-Tactile Diffusion Policy for end-to-end execution. We conduct comprehensive ablation studies exploring different VLM-based task planners to identify optimal demonstration generation pipelines, and systematically compare imitation learning algorithms for skill distillation. Extensive simulation experiments and physical deployment validate that our approach achieves policy learning for long-horizon manipulation without costly human demonstrations, while the VLM-guided atomic skill framework enables scalable generalization to diverse tasks.

keyInsights: Synthetically generated expert demonstrations, explicitly incorporating force-awareness, can be more effective and efficient than human-collected data for learning gentle and precise robotic manipulation.
Decomposing complex, long-horizon tasks into a sequence of robust, force-aware atomic skills, guided by a VLM, significantly enhances the stability and generalizability of robot policies.
Fusing visual and tactile point cloud data provides a comprehensive and robust perceptual input, crucial for nuanced contact-rich interactions that purely visual methods cannot reliably capture.
originalProblem: Traditional robotic manipulation methods rely on extensive, costly, and labor-intensive human demonstrations, limiting scalability and generalization.
Existing robotic systems often lack fine-grained contact force awareness, leading to non-gentle interactions and potential object damage during delicate tasks.

Integrating Visual Language Models (VLMs) for high-level task planning often overlooks the crucial physical interaction details, such as tactile and contact force information, hindering robust manipulation in contact-rich environments.
results: Individual force-aware atomic skills achieved high success rates (0.89-0.98) in simulation, and the VLM planner demonstrated an average success rate of 0.713 in generating correct skill sequences.

Policies learned from VASK-generated data consistently outperformed those trained on human demonstrations, achieving significantly higher success rates (e.g., 0.90 vs. 0.30 for Object Stack) and substantially lower average contact forces (e.g., 0.322N vs. 0.809N).

The multimodal VT-DP, utilizing both visual and tactile point clouds, achieved the highest average task success rate of 0.79 across benchmark tasks, outperforming diffusion policies based solely on visual point clouds (0.58) or RGB images (0.28).
solution: A library of force-aware 'atomic skills' is acquired using Reinforcement Learning (Soft Actor-Critic) in simulation, where each skill's reward function explicitly includes force penalty terms to encourage gentle interaction.

A Visual Language with Atomic Skills (VASK) framework automatically generates high-quality, long-horizon expert demonstrations in simulation by having a VLM plan sequences of these force-aware atomic skills.

A Visual-Tactile Diffusion Policy (VT-DP) is trained to distill policies from the VASK-generated multimodal data (visual and tactile point clouds), enabling robust end-to-end execution of gentle manipulation tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Harbin Institute of Technology (Shenzhen) and Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou) developed a framework for teaching robots gentle, long-horizon manipulation without human demonstrations. Their system combines force-aware atomic skills, VLM-guided synthetic data generation, and a visual-tactile diffusion policy, achieving higher task success rates and lower contact forces compared to policies trained on human-collected data.

title: Gentle Manipulation Policy Learning via Demonstrations from VLM Planned Atomic Skills

abstract:
We introduce Behavior from Language and Demonstration (BLADE), a framework
for long-horizon robotic manipulation by integrating imitation learning and
model-based planning. BLADE leverages language-annotated demonstrations,
extracts abstract action knowledge from large language models (LLMs), and
constructs a library of structured, high-level action representations. These
representations include preconditions and effects grounded in visual perception
for each high-level action, along with corresponding controllers implemented as
neural network-based policies. BLADE can recover such structured
representations automatically, without manually labeled states or symbolic
definitions. BLADE shows significant capabilities in generalizing to novel
situations, including novel initial states, external state perturbations, and
novel goals. We validate the effectiveness of our approach both in simulation
and on real robots with a diverse set of objects with articulated parts,
partial observability, and geometric constraints.

keyInsights: Combining low-level mechanical understanding from demonstrations with high-level abstract understanding from LLMs enables automatic recovery of structured action representations.
Abstract predicate truth values can be automatically annotated across demonstration sequences based on LLM-generated behavior descriptions, eliminating manual labeling for classifier training.
A bi-level planning and execution loop, where the planner re-evaluates after each low-level action, provides robustness to real-world uncertainties and state perturbations.
originalProblem: Imitation learning struggles with generalization to novel states and lacks compositional ability for long-horizon tasks.
Model-based planning requires extensive manual effort to define symbolic action representations (preconditions, effects) and struggles with grounding these in high-dimensional perception.
Existing methods using LLMs for robotics often lack robust grounding in the physical world, leading to infeasible or hallucinated actions.
results: In simulation (CALVIN benchmark), BLADE showed over 65% improvement in "Abstract Goal" task success compared to hierarchical policy HULC and outperformed other LLM-based baselines.
The automatic predicate annotation method improved visual classifier F1 scores by 20.7%, particularly for spatial relations (38.6%), directly boosting planning success rates.

On a real robot, BLADE successfully completed at least 4 out of 6 tasks across all generalization types (unseen initial, perturbation, constraints, partial observability) in two complex manipulation domains, unlike baselines that often failed.
solution: BLADE automatically generates PDDL-like symbolic behavior descriptions (preconditions, effects, primitive sequences) by leveraging LLMs and language-annotated human demonstrations.
It introduces an automatic predicate annotation method to train visual state classifiers, grounding abstract predicates in raw observations without manual labeling.
A bi-level planning and execution strategy utilizes these learned abstractions for symbolic planning, followed by execution via trained visuomotor policies and robust replanning.
paper_summary.summary: The BLADE framework enables robots to learn compositional, long-horizon manipulation behaviors by automatically acquiring structured action models (preconditions and effects) from language-annotated demonstrations and large language models. It grounds these abstract representations in visual perception, allowing robots to generalize to novel initial states, external perturbations, and new task compositions.

title: Learning Compositional Behaviors from Demonstration and Language

abstract: Developing general-purpose navigation policies for unknown environments remains a core challenge in robotics. Most existing systems rely on task-specific neural networks and fixed information flows, limiting their generalizability. Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) offer a promising alternative by embedding human-like knowledge for reasoning and planning, but prior LVLM-robot integrations have largely depended on pre-mapped spaces, hard-coded representations, and rigid control logic. We introduce the Agentic Robotic Navigation Architecture (ARNA), a general-purpose framework that equips an LVLM-based agent with a library of perception, reasoning, and navigation tools drawn from modern robotic stacks. At runtime, the agent autonomously defines and executes task-specific workflows that iteratively query modules, reason over multimodal inputs, and select navigation actions. This agentic formulation enables robust navigation and reasoning in previously unmapped environments, offering a new perspective on robotic stack design. Evaluated in Habitat Lab on the HM-EQA benchmark, ARNA outperforms state-of-the-art EQA-specific approaches. Qualitative results on RxR and custom tasks further demonstrate its ability to generalize across a broad range of navigation challenges.

keyInsights: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) can serve as effective orchestrators for entire robotic stacks, dynamically generating task-specific workflows to replace rigid, expert-designed pipelines.

Grounding LVLM reasoning with multimodal memory, including a 3D scene graph and an occupancy grid map, is critical for efficient spatial awareness, preventing hallucinations, and enabling targeted exploration.
A structured, self-discover-inspired approach for workflow generation significantly improves decision-making and tool management compared to simpler Chain-of-Thought prompting.
originalProblem: Traditional robotic navigation systems rely on rigid, handcrafted pipelines, limiting their adaptability to novel tasks or unknown environments.

Previous integrations of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) in robotics were restricted by fixed workflows and simplified environmental representations, leading to myopic reasoning and poor generalization.
Robots lacked the ability to dynamically reason over abstract, natural language instructions while fully utilizing the capabilities of modern perception and planning stacks.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the HM-EQA benchmark, with 77% accuracy, surpassing the best active exploration baseline (EuC-GPT-4o) by a margin of 16 percentage points.
Demonstrated superior exploration efficiency with a mean path length of 16.55 meters on HM-EQA, significantly shorter than baselines (e.g., EuC-GPT-4o at 175.91m).
Ablation studies confirmed that multimodal representations (scene graph, occupancy grid map) and structured workflow generation are critical for accuracy and efficient exploration.
Showcased qualitative generalization to complex tasks like RxR-CE, achieving a 12% success rate on 30 episodes, and custom open-ended navigation problems in unmapped environments.
solution: Introduced the Agentic Robotic Navigation Architecture (ARNA), where an LVLM acts as an agentic controller to autonomously define and execute task-specific workflows.

Provided the LVLM agent with a comprehensive toolset for just-in-time access to robotic capabilities, including perception, reasoning utilities (e.g., scene graph queries, shortest path), and navigation controls.
Implemented a multimodal memory system, including textual descriptions and a rasterized occupancy grid with landmarks, which the LVLM directly controls and updates for grounded reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Stanford University and JPL researchers developed the Agentic Robotic Navigation Architecture (ARNA), which employs a Large Vision-Language Model to dynamically orchestrate a comprehensive robotic stack for general-purpose navigation in unmapped environments. The system achieved 77% accuracy and a mean path length of 16.55 meters on the HM-EQA benchmark, surpassing existing baselines while demonstrating adaptability to diverse natural language tasks.

title: General-Purpose Robotic Navigation via LVLM-Orchestrated Perception, Reasoning, and Acting

abstract: Learning robust robotic control policies remains a major challenge due to the high cost of collecting labeled data, limited generalization to unseen environments, and difficulties in planning over long horizons. While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a promising solution by grounding natural language instructions into single-step control commands, they often lack mechanisms for lookahead and struggle with compounding errors in dynamic tasks. In this project, we introduce Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs (SITCOM), a framework that augments any pretrained VLA with model-based rollouts and reward-based trajectory selection, inspired by Model Predictive Control algorithm. SITCOM leverages a learned dynamics model to simulate multi-step action rollouts to select the best candidate plan for real-world execution, transforming one-shot VLAs into robust long-horizon planners. We develop an efficient transformer-based dynamics model trained on large-scale BridgeV2 data and fine-tuned on SIMPLER environments to bridge the Real2Sim gap, and score candidate rollouts using rewards from simulator. Through comprehensive evaluation across multiple tasks and settings in the SIMPLER environment, we demonstrate that SITCOM when combined with a good reward function can significantly improve task completion rate from 48% to 72% using trained dynamics model.

keyInsights: Integrating model-based planning significantly transforms reactive, single-step VLAs into proactive, long-horizon planners, crucial for complex robotic tasks.
Domain adaptation through fine-tuning is essential for both the core VLA policy and the learned dynamics model to effectively bridge the "Real2Sim" gap and ensure practical performance.

Scaling inference-time compute by increasing the breadth (number of candidate rollouts) and depth (length of rollouts) can yield continuous performance gains for challenging tasks, despite potential computational increases.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily operate in a "one-shot" manner, predicting only the immediate next action without explicit lookahead or planning capabilities.
This lack of foresight leads to compounding errors in dynamic environments and limits the ability of VLAs to execute complex, multi-step tasks requiring long-horizon planning.
Traditional robot control policies struggle with high data collection costs and poor generalization to novel environments.
results: SITCOM (World Model) achieved an average success rate of 72% in SIMPLER manipulation tasks, a significant improvement over the fine-tuned OpenVLA-SFT (48%) and the baseline OpenVLA (1%).

Fine-tuning the dynamics model on in-domain SIMPLER trajectories reduced its Frechet Inception Distance (FID) from 17.0 to 11.2 and Optical Flow Loss (OFL) from 1.665 to 0.992, indicating improved state prediction.

The DAgger-inspired training strategy for the dynamics model effectively improved reconstruction quality and reduced prediction drift over longer horizons compared to single-timestep prediction training.
solution: SITCOM introduces an inference-time planning framework that augments pre-trained VLAs with model-based rollouts and reward-ranking capabilities, inspired by Model Predictive Control.

An efficient transformer-based dynamics model is developed to simulate multi-step action sequences, pre-trained on BridgeV2 and fine-tuned on target environments (SIMPLER) with a DAgger-inspired adaptation strategy to mitigate error accumulation.

The framework samples candidate actions, simulates their future trajectories using the dynamics model, evaluates trajectories with a reward model, and executes only the first action of the highest-scoring plan in a receding horizon loop.
paper_summary.summary: The SITCOM framework from Carnegie Mellon University enhances Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by integrating an inference-time model-based planning mechanism. This system leverages a learned transformer-based dynamics model for multi-step action rollouts, achieving an average success rate of 72% on various simulated robotic manipulation tasks and substantially outperforming reactive VLA baselines.

title: SITCOM: Scaling Inference-Time COMpute for VLAs

abstract: Classical robotic systems typically rely on custom planners designed for constrained environments. While effective in restricted settings, these systems lack generalization capabilities, limiting the scalability of embodied AI and general-purpose robots. Recent data-driven Vision-Language-Action (VLA) approaches aim to learn policies from large-scale simulation and real-world data. However, the continuous action space of the physical world significantly exceeds the representational capacity of linguistic tokens, making it unclear if scaling data alone can yield general robotic intelligence. To address this gap, we propose ActionReasoning, an LLM-driven framework that performs explicit action reasoning to produce physics-consistent, prior-guided decisions for robotic manipulation. ActionReasoning leverages the physical priors and real-world knowledge already encoded in Large Language Models (LLMs) and structures them within a multi-agent architecture. We instantiate this framework on a tractable case study of brick stacking, where the environment states are assumed to be already accurately measured. The environmental states are then serialized and passed to a multi-agent LLM framework that generates physics-aware action plans. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed multi-agent LLM framework enables stable brick placement while shifting effort from low-level domain-specific coding to high-level tool invocation and prompting, highlighting its potential for broader generalization. This work introduces a promising approach to bridging perception and execution in robotic manipulation by integrating physical reasoning with LLMs.

keyInsights: Integrating LLMs with explicit 3D world model inputs and physics-based verification enables robust and accurate physical reasoning, surpassing the limitations of purely data-driven or 2D-only approaches.

A multi-stage, multi-agent architecture with explicit gating between phases is critical for complex, long-horizon tasks, as it prevents the propagation of early errors and ensures robust task completion.

Shifting robotic development from intricate low-level coding to high-level tool invocation and strategic prompting simplifies robot deployment and enhances adaptability to varying environments and tasks.
originalProblem: Traditional robotic manipulation systems require extensive domain-specific engineering, limiting scalability and versatility for new tasks and configurations.

Current data-driven Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models struggle with the continuous and vast action space of the physical world, making general robotic intelligence through data scaling alone uncertain.
Existing LLM-based robot systems often rely on 2D keypoint identification, which suffers from occlusions, foreshortening, and a lack of precise 3D understanding for robust physical interaction.
results: Reduced mean rotation error by 30.0% (to 0.703°) and mean center-offset error by 85.2% (to 0.637 cm) compared to a classical controller baseline in brick-stacking tasks.
Achieved a 129% improvement in 3D Intersection-over-Union (to 0.8803) for placed bricks, resulting in significantly neater and more stable stacked structures.

An ablation study demonstrated that a single-agent LLM variant consistently underperformed the multi-agent pipeline, frequently failing to complete the stacking task due to error propagation and lack of inter-stage verification.
solution: A multi-agent LLM architecture that takes structured 3D environment states and high-level task goals to generate precise SE(3) target poses for robot end-effector control.

The system operates in a Markovian waypoint loop, framing action selection as a physics-guided inverse problem where candidate actions are verified against constraints like collision avoidance and reachability.

Employs a sequential, gated pipeline of six specialized LLM agents, each instantiated via structured prompts with environment state, task memory, role definitions, and callable tools to handle distinct manipulation phases.
paper_summary.summary: ActionReasoning introduces a multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) framework for robust 3D robotic action reasoning, demonstrating improved geometric accuracy and stability in complex brick-stacking tasks. This framework achieved an 85.2% reduction in center offset error and a 129% improvement in 3D IoU compared to classical controllers, by leveraging structured world model inputs and physics-guided decision-making.

title: ActionReasoning: Robot Action Reasoning in 3D Space with LLM for Robotic Brick Stacking

abstract:
Existing robot policies predominantly adopt the task-centric approach,
requiring end-to-end task data collection. This results in limited
generalization to new tasks and difficulties in pinpointing errors within
long-horizon, multi-stage tasks. To address this, we propose RoboMatrix, a
skill-centric hierarchical framework designed for scalable robot task planning
and execution in open-world environments. RoboMatrix extracts general
meta-skills from diverse complex tasks, enabling the completion of unseen tasks
through skill composition. Its architecture consists of a high-level scheduling
layer that utilizes large language models (LLMs) for task decomposition, an
intermediate skill layer housing meta-skill models, and a low-level hardware
layer for robot control. A key innovation of our work is the introduction of
the first unified vision-language-action (VLA) model capable of seamlessly
integrating both movement and manipulation within one model. This is achieved
by combining vision and language prompts to generate discrete actions.
Experimental results demonstrate that RoboMatrix achieves a 50% higher success
rate than task-centric baselines when applied to unseen objects, scenes, and
tasks. To advance open-world robotics research, we will open-source code,
hardware designs, model weights, and datasets at
this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Decomposing complex tasks into generalizable meta-skills significantly improves robot generalization to novel objects, scenes, and task compositions, while reducing overall data requirements.
A unified VLA model can effectively learn and execute a broad range of discrete, atomic skills by leveraging multimodal alignment pre-training and supervised fine-tuning on fine-grained skill data.

A hierarchical architecture, with an LLM for task decomposition and an execution checker for validating subtask feasibility, enhances interpretability and robustness, crucial for deployment in dynamic, open-world settings.
originalProblem: Existing end-to-end robot policies, even with advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs), struggle to generalize effectively to new tasks, unseen objects, or novel environmental configurations.
Traditional task-centric approaches require extensive data collection and re-training for each new complex task, leading to exponential data demands and poor scalability.
The 'black-box' nature of end-to-end learning makes it difficult to diagnose errors and pinpoint failures in long-horizon, multi-stage robotic tasks.
results: RoboMatrix achieved 90-100% success rates on meta-skills and outperformed task-centric baselines by up to 50% in Level V generalization scenarios involving novel objects, scenes, and task compositions.

The skill-centric method demonstrated an 80% success rate on hard-level long-horizon tasks (over 5 steps in unseen scenarios), a 40% improvement over task-centric methods, and showed robustness to dynamic human interferences.

Multimodal pre-training and domain-specific alignment significantly improved VLA model performance, boosting overall success on a sequential long-horizon task from 30% to 100%, with larger model sizes (13B vs. 7B) consistently yielding higher success rates.
solution: A skill-centric pipeline that defines a finite set of reusable 'meta-skills' and uses an iterative data engine for efficient, continuous collection and refinement of skill-specific training data.

A dual-model Skill Layer featuring a unified Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that integrates movement and manipulation, based on a decode-only LLM (Vicuna 1.5) and a visual encoder (CLIP), alongside hybrid models for specific deterministic skills.

A hierarchical framework comprising a GPT-powered Modular Scheduling Layer for high-level task planning and execution checking, a Skill Layer for action generation, and a Hardware Layer for physical control and real-time state observation.
paper_summary.summary: RoboMatrix introduces a skill-centric hierarchical framework that enhances robot generalization, data efficiency, and robustness for task planning and execution in open-world environments. This approach decomposes complex tasks into reusable meta-skills, allowing robots to adapt to novel objects, scenes, and dynamic interactions.

title: RoboMatrix: A Skill-centric Hierarchical Framework for Scalable Robot  Task Planning and Execution in Open-World

abstract: In embodied artificial intelligence, enabling heterogeneous robot teams to execute long-horizon tasks from high-level instructions remains a critical challenge. While large language models (LLMs) show promise in instruction parsing and preliminary planning, they exhibit limitations in long-term reasoning and dynamic multi-robot coordination. We propose EmboTeam, a novel embodied multi-robot task planning framework that addresses these issues through a three-stage cascaded architecture: 1) It leverages an LLM to parse instructions and generate Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) problem descriptions, thereby transforming commands into formal planning problems; 2) It combines the semantic reasoning of LLMs with the search capabilities of a classical planner to produce optimized action sequences; 3) It compiles the resulting plan into behavior trees for reactive control. The framework supports dynamically sized heterogeneous robot teams via a shared blackboard mechanism for communication and state synchronization. To validate our approach, we introduce the MACE-THOR benchmark dataset, comprising 42 complex tasks across 8 distinct household layouts. Experiments show EmboTeam improves the task success rate from 12% to 55% and goal condition recall from 32% to 72% over the LaMMA-P baseline.

keyInsights: A neuro-symbolic integration approach effectively combines the semantic understanding of LLMs with the logical rigor of formal planners and the reactivity of Behavior Trees for robust multi-robot task execution.
Co-optimizing task decomposition and robot allocation using an LLM, followed by semantic merging of sub-plans, significantly improves global plan coherence and inter-robot coordination.

Translating linear plans into hierarchical Behavior Trees with integrated condition checks, recovery mechanisms, and synchronization nodes allows for effective fault tolerance and local adaptability during execution in dynamic environments.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM)-based robotic planning systems often struggle with long-term reasoning, dynamic multi-robot coordination, and handling complex interdependencies.
Traditional multi-robot task planning methods face computational and scalability challenges, with limited generalization across complex tasks and dynamic environments.
There is a need to seamlessly bridge high-level natural language instructions into robust, parallelizable, and fault-tolerant physical actions for heterogeneous robot teams.
results: EmboTeam achieved an aggregate Success Rate of 55% and Goal Condition Recall of 72% on the MACE-THOR benchmark, demonstrating an absolute improvement of 43% SR and 40% GCR compared to LaMMA-P.

The framework showed superior performance in both Parallel-Independent (SR 0.71, GCR 0.88) and Temporal-Dependent (SR 0.38, GCR 0.62) tasks, indicating its effectiveness in managing complex multi-robot collaboration and synchronization.

Ablation studies confirmed that all components (PFG, HP, BTC) are crucial for the framework's performance, with their integration leading to optimal success rates and robustness against execution failures.
solution: EmboTeam proposes a three-stage cascaded architecture: a PDDL File Generator (PFG), a Hybrid Planner (HP), and a Behavior Tree Compiler (BTC).
The PFG uses an LLM for co-optimized task decomposition and sub-task allocation, generating PDDL problems that ensure atomicity, skill matching, and parallelism.

The HP combines LLM semantic validation and merging with a classical planner to generate globally optimal and conflict-free action sequences, which are then compiled by the BTC into parallel, reactive behavior trees with embedded 'Precondition-Execution-Validation' mechanisms for robustness.
paper_summary.summary: EmboTeam introduces a three-stage cascaded architecture that integrates Large Language Models, PDDL planners, and reactive Behavior Trees to enable heterogeneous multi-robot teams to execute complex, long-horizon tasks from natural language instructions. The framework achieved an aggregate success rate of 55% and goal condition recall of 72% on the MACE-THOR benchmark, outperforming baseline methods by 43% in success rate and 40% in goal condition recall.

title: EmboTeam: Grounding LLM Reasoning into Reactive Behavior Trees via PDDL for Embodied Multi-Robot Collaboration

abstract: Language-guided long-horizon manipulation of deformable objects presents significant challenges due to high degrees of freedom, complex dynamics, and the need for accurate vision-language grounding. In this work, we focus on multi-step cloth folding, a representative deformable-object manipulation task that requires both structured long-horizon planning and fine-grained visual perception. To this end, we propose a unified framework that integrates a Large Language Model (LLM)-based planner, a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based perception system, and a task execution module. Specifically, the LLM-based planner decomposes high-level language instructions into low-level action primitives, bridging the semantic-execution gap, aligning perception with action, and enhancing generalization. The VLM-based perception module employs a SigLIP2-driven architecture with a bidirectional cross-attention fusion mechanism and weight-decomposed low-rank adaptation (DoRA) fine-tuning to achieve language-conditioned fine-grained visual grounding. Experiments in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate the method's effectiveness. In simulation, it outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 2.23, 1.87, and 33.3 on seen instructions, unseen instructions, and unseen tasks, respectively. On a real robot, it robustly executes multi-step folding sequences from language instructions across diverse cloth materials and configurations, demonstrating strong generalization in practical scenarios. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: The synergistic integration of LLM's high-level compositional reasoning with VLM's fine-grained visual grounding effectively bridges the semantic-execution gap in complex deformable object manipulation.

Strategic application of parameter-efficient tuning methods, specifically DoRA, is crucial for adapting large, pre-trained VLMs to specialized deformable object tasks while efficiently preserving general knowledge.

A novel bidirectional cross-attention mechanism significantly enhances the alignment between segmented language instructions and visual features, leading to more accurate determination of manipulation points on deformable objects.
originalProblem: Manipulating deformable objects like cloth is challenging due to their high degrees of freedom, complex dynamics, and pervasive self-occlusion, particularly for multi-step tasks such as folding.
Current VLM-based language-guided manipulation systems often treat language as a static goal descriptor and struggle with interpreting complex, multi-step instructions over long task horizons.
Existing LLM applications in robotics primarily focus on rigid object domains, lacking sufficient validation and efficacy in visually ambiguous and physically deformable scenarios.
results: The proposed method consistently outperformed state-of-the-art baselines in simulation, achieving 91.7% success for seen instructions, 89.6% for unseen instructions, and 77.92% for unseen tasks on complex cloth folding.

Real-world experiments on a UR5 robot demonstrated robust sim-to-real transfer, achieving average success rates of 88% (seen instructions), 84% (unseen instructions), and 68% (unseen tasks) across 12 diverse fabric types.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of the SigLIP2 VLM backbone, the bidirectional cross-attention fusion strategy, DoRA for parameter-efficient tuning, and GPT-4o for robust task planning.
solution: A modular framework was developed, integrating Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR), an LLM-based task planner (GPT-4o), a VLM-based visual perception module (SigLIP2), and an action executor for pick-and-place operations.
The LLM planner, guided by In-Context Instruction Learning and Chain-of-Thought reasoning, decomposes high-level commands into a sequence of standardized, robot-executable pick-and-place sub-tasks.

The VLM leverages Grounding DINO and SAM for segmentation, and a DoRA-tuned SigLIP2 model with a novel bidirectional cross-attention mechanism to achieve fine-grained, language-conditioned visual grounding of manipulation points.
paper_summary.summary: This research presents a unified framework for language-guided, long-horizon manipulation of deformable objects, specifically focusing on multi-step cloth folding. The system integrates LLM-based task planning with VLM-based visual perception to enable robots to understand and execute natural language commands. It achieved superior generalization and robustness in both simulation (up to 91.7% success for seen instructions, 77.92% for unseen tasks) and real-world settings (up to 88% for seen instructions, 68% for unseen tasks) compared to existing methods.

title: Language-Guided Long Horizon Manipulation with LLM-based Planning and Visual Perception

abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are successful in completing various language processing tasks, they easily fail to interact with the physical world by generating control sequences properly. We find that the main reason is that LLMs are not grounded in the physical world. Existing LLM-based approaches circumvent this problem by relying on additional pre-defined skills or pre-trained sub-policies, making it hard to adapt to new tasks. In contrast, we aim to address this problem and explore the possibility to prompt pre-trained LLMs to accomplish a series of robotic manipulation tasks in a training-free paradigm. Accordingly, we propose a framework called LLM+A(ffordance) where the LLM serves as both the sub-task planner (that generates high-level plans) and the motion controller (that generates low-level control sequences). To ground these plans and control sequences on the physical world, we develop the affordance prompting technique that stimulates the LLM to 1) predict the consequences of generated plans and 2) generate affordance values for relevant objects. Empirically, we evaluate the effectiveness of LLM+A in various language-conditioned robotic manipulation tasks, which show that our approach substantially improves performance by enhancing the feasibility of generated plans and control and can easily generalize to different environments.
title: Empowering Large Language Models on Robotic Manipulation with Affordance Prompting

abstract: While vision-language-action (VLA) models for embodied agents integrate perception, reasoning, and control, they remain constrained by two critical weaknesses: first, during grasping tasks, the action tokens generated by the language model often exhibit subtle spatial deviations from the target object, resulting in grasp failures; second, they lack the ability to reliably recognize task completion, which leads to redundant actions and frequent timeout errors. To address these challenges and enhance robustness, we propose a lightweight, training-free framework, VLA-SCT. This framework operates as a self-correcting control loop, combining data-driven action refinement with conditional logic for termination. Consequently, compared to baseline approaches, our method achieves consistent improvements across all datasets in the LIBERO benchmark, significantly increasing the success rate of fine manipulation tasks and ensuring accurate task completion, thereby promoting the deployment of more reliable VLA agents in complex, unstructured environments.

keyInsights: A training-free, external feedback loop can effectively address precision and termination issues in pre-trained VLA models without modifying their core architecture.
Evaluating motion quality through geometric and kinematic metrics provides an early signal for detecting and correcting suboptimal robot trajectories.
Dynamic perturbation generation, guided by historical successful actions in similar visual contexts, enhances manipulation precision and task success.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often lack precision in fine-grained manipulation, leading to grasp failures and unstable operations.
Many VLA models struggle with reliable task termination, frequently performing superfluous actions and resulting in timeout errors.
These limitations create a "knowledge-action gap," hindering the reliable deployment of VLA models in real-world scenarios.
results: VLA-SCT achieved a state-of-the-art average success rate of 81.55% on the LIBERO benchmark, representing a 6.1% absolute improvement over the OpenVLA baseline (75.45%).
The framework demonstrated consistent superior performance across all four LIBERO task categories while providing a 1.12x inference speedup over the baseline.

Ablation studies confirmed that both the self-correction mechanism and the termination detection module individually contribute to significant success rate improvements (4.2% and 2.4% respectively), with synergistic benefits when combined.
solution: Introduces VLA-SCT (Self-Correction and Termination), a lightweight, training-free framework that acts as a modular intelligent control layer for existing VLA models.
Incorporates a Trajectory Evaluation module to assess motion quality and a Grasp Perturbation module to generate targeted action corrections based on successful historical actions.
Employs a Termination Detection module that compares the current visual state with a repository of successful states using Pearson correlation to recognize task completion.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Ping An Technology developed VLA-SCT, a framework enhancing Vision-Language-Action models by improving manipulation precision and task termination. It achieved an 81.55% average success rate on the LIBERO benchmark, an absolute improvement of 6.1% over the OpenVLA baseline, while also demonstrating a 1.12x inference speedup.

title: From Knowing to Doing Precisely: A General Self-Correction and Termination Framework for VLA models
